Jessica wheeled herself over to the small sitting area, flipping open her own sleek corporate laptop to review emails. Kristin stretched out on the far queen bed, flipping through the channels on the hotel television with the volume kept low.

I stood up from the mattress, my muscles delightfully heavy from the hydrotherapy and the massive influx of calories. I walked over to the large, polished mahogany desk sitting opposite the beds.

It was time to fully deploy the command center.

I reached into my padded bag and grabbed the 17-inch piano-black rig. After a long day of transit and the physical toll my cerebral palsy-affected muscles had taken from carrying Jessica, lifting the massive "desktop replacement" laptop felt like hoisting a cinder block. It weighed nearly ten pounds, a sprawling slab of premium 2006 hardware. I carefully set it down on the desk, flipping the heavy screen open.

Unlike the cramped, fold-out tray on the ARTS *Cascades* train, this hotel desk was sprawling. I had massive amounts of clearance. I plugged in the weighted optical mouse, the red laser glowing sharply against the wood grain.

I booted the system, the *Quantum OS Clarity* desktop flaring to life. The *Denizens 2* disc was already sitting in the optical drive. I double-clicked the icon, passed the SecuROM check, and let the high-definition simulation load.

The second the cheerful, quirky loading music drifted from the laptop's premium speakers, I heard the scrape of wooden legs against the carpet.

Rachel had dragged one of the heavy, upholstered hotel chairs right next to mine. She sat down, instantly hooking her left arm securely around my waist. She leaned her entire body weight flush against my right side and rested her chin heavily on my shoulder. Her separation anxiety was no longer driving her into a frantic panic, but the physical tether was her absolute safe zone. She needed to be locked in, watching her Bear construct his world.

"Room to breathe?" Rachel rasped softly, noting the wide expanse of the hotel desk.

"Plenty of room," I smiled, wrapping my right arm around her back and giving her a tight, one-armed squeeze before returning my hand to the mouse.

The digital neighborhood loaded. I clicked on the family bin, pulling up the household I had started building back in Renton. The avatars of Bug, Jessica, and Ashley were waiting in the queue.

But my analytical processor wasn't satisfied with my first pass.

"What are you doing?" Rachel asked as I bypassed the 'Move In' button and instead pressed a highly specific combination of keys on the keyboard.

A small, translucent gray command console dropped down from the top edge of the screen.

"I need to refine the architecture," I explained, my fingers flying across the keys. I typed in the developer-level cheat string: *bool_prop edit_citizen true*. I hit enter, and the console vanished.

Holding down the shift key, I clicked directly on the digital version of Bug. A hidden pie-menu popped up, filled with raw data strings and debug commands. I selected *Force Create-A-Character*.

The game paused, the background dissolving back into the familiar, blue-lit pedestal room. The digital Bug avatar appeared, ready to be edited.

"You're tweaking her?" Rachel asked, her arm tightening around my ribs in a heavy, affectionate hug.

"I have to get it right," I said, my eyes completely locked onto the screen.

With the spatial freedom of the mouse, I was able to navigate the complex sliders with total precision. I zoomed in on the avatar's face. I adjusted the bridge of the wire-rimmed glasses, ensuring they sat perfectly. I tweaked the jawline just a fraction of a millimeter, making it slightly sharper, perfectly capturing the fierce, unyielding determination my sister had shown sitting in the diner that morning.

Then, I navigated to the clothing tabs. I found the exact, signature bright red t-shirt that mirrored her uniform from The Spot. I adjusted the texture, ensuring the digital fabric looked crisp and authentic.

"She looks exactly like me," Rachel whispered, a profound warmth radiating from her voice as she watched the digital clone spin on the pedestal.

I didn't just hit save. I moved my left hand to the keyboard and rested my finger over the 'C' key.

I rotated the avatar into a strong, confident pose and pressed the key.

*Click-whir.* The game's simulated camera shutter sound echoed through the speakers, capturing a high-resolution screenshot of the Bug and saving it directly to the hard drive's directory. I zoomed in on her face and took another. *Click.* I zoomed out for a full-body shot. *Click.* "What are the pictures for?" Rachel asked, resting her cheek against my collarbone.

"The website," I explained, minimizing the game for a second to bring up the *Vertex Creative Studio* HTML file I had started coding on the train. I pointed to a blank image tag sitting in the code. "I can't just write the lore. I need visual documentation. I'm going to pull the screenshots from the game's directory, crop them in the photo editor, and embed them directly into the story."

Rachel looked at the raw HTML code, and then back at the game. She understood the depth of the project instantly. I wasn't just playing a video game; I was actively, meticulously documenting our survival and rebuilding our legacy in a digital format where Delores could never touch it.

Rachel let out a long, happy sigh, burying her face into my neck and giving me another heavy, crushing hug.

"Build the Bear next," she demanded quietly, refusing to let go.

"You got it," I promised, maximizing the game window and preparing to drop myself into the sanctuary.

### Part 8: The Final Baseline – 10:00 PM (PST)

By the time I finished meticulously crafting the digital avatar of myself in *The Denizens 2* and embedding the screenshots into the *Vertex Creative Studio* lore document, the adrenaline that had been keeping my processor online finally flatlined.

The heavy, residual heat from the hydrotherapy pool, combined with the massive influx of calories from the room service feast, hit the pack like a localized gravity well.

"Command center offline," Jessica announced from the sitting area, her voice thick with exhaustion as she closed the lid of her corporate laptop. "We have a 6:00 AM wake-up call tomorrow. Transit math requires us to be fully rested before we march to the Rose City Center for the broadcast."

I saved the HTML architecture, backed up the neighborhood files, and powered down the massive 17-inch rig. The fans spun down, leaving the hotel room in a heavy, insulated silence.

The physical geometry of the room needed to be configured for sleep.

We had two queen beds. Because Jessica required a specific perimeter and physical clearance to safely transfer from her wheelchair in the middle of the night, she claimed the bed closest to the bathroom. That left the second queen bed, situated near the heavy blackout curtains of the window, for the rest of the pack.

The zero-privacy baseline we had established earlier that afternoon was about to hit its absolute, logical conclusion. We were simply too exhausted to dig through our duffel bags to change out of our comfortable loungewear and into designated sleepwear.

I stood beside the bed, Rachel directly in front of me.

"No armor," Rachel rasped softly, her dark eyes completely clear of the frantic separation anxiety that had plagued her since Thursday.

"No armor," I agreed.

We stripped off our heavy flannel and sleep shirts, dropping them onto the carpet. Kristin stood up from the edge of the mattress and did the exact same thing. We didn't stop at our shirts. We stripped down to our bare skin.

For two years, my biological defense mechanism had been violently conditioned to equate female proximity and physical vulnerability with absolute, predatory danger. Delores and Brandi had turned the female body into a weapon of degradation. Stripping away all of my physical armor in a room with three women should have sent my trauma-wired processor into a catastrophic, blinding Redline.

But as I stood there in the soft, dim light of the hotel lamps, my threat-detection system remained entirely dormant. There was no panic. There was only my older sister, my fiercely loyal Bug, and the sentinel who had sworn to protect us. The weaponization of the female body had been completely and permanently dismantled.

Rachel climbed into the bed first, taking the side nearest the window. I climbed in next, taking the direct center of the mattress. Rachel immediately plastered herself against my right side, throwing her bare leg over mine and wrapping her arm securely across my chest. Skin-to-skin contact provided the ultimate, undeniable sensory data that her Bear was real and present.

Then, the mattress shifted on my left.

Kristin climbed under the heavy hotel comforter. She didn't stay on the far edge. She slid right up against my left side, her bare shoulder pressing flush against mine, her arm resting casually across my stomach to complete the physical perimeter.

I was literally sandwiched in the center of the bed, entirely boxed in by absolute safety.

Jessica clicked off the main room lights, leaving only the soft glow of the digital clock on the nightstand.

For a few minutes, the only sound in the room was the quiet hum of the air conditioner. But then, in the darkness, I felt Rachel's chest begin to shake against my ribs.

A quiet, highly amused giggle escaped her lips.

"What's funny?" I whispered, my voice heavy with sleep.

"I was just thinking," Rachel whispered back, her voice dancing with vindictive joy. "If Delores could somehow magically see through that door right now... her head would literally detach from her neck and explode."

A low, rumbling chuckle started deep in my chest.

Delores’s paranoia regarding my family wasn't just toxic; it was clinically insane. She couldn't comprehend a pure, fiercely protective, platonic bond. She had spent two years projecting the most vile, deeply twisted accusations onto us. She genuinely believed—and routinely screamed at me—that I was engaged in wildly inappropriate relationships with Jessica, Rachel, and even our cousins Janette, Maria, and Katherine.

"She seriously thought you were sleeping with your cousins?" Kristin asked from my left, her voice a mix of disgust and sheer disbelief.

"Oh, absolutely," Jessica chimed in from the other bed in the dark. "She thought our entire family was just a giant, incestuous threat to her territory. She couldn't handle the fact that Michael's primary anchors were women who actually loved him."

"So," Rachel giggled, tightening her arm across my chest. "If she saw her hostage sleeping completely naked, wrapped up in the arms of his little sister..."

"She would burn the entire hotel down," I laughed, the absurdity of the monster's delusions finally stripped of all their power.

"Wait," Kristin said, shifting slightly against my left side. I could hear the sharp, intelligent smirk in her voice. "It's even worse than that. Because Delores knows I'm not related to you."

Rachel gasped in the dark, a fresh wave of laughter hitting her. "Oh my god. You're right."

"Exactly," Kristin continued, lightly tapping her fingers against my ribs. "I'm the roommate. I'm not blood. Technically speaking, as two unrelated consenting adults currently sharing a bed with zero clothes on, we *could* actually be doing exactly what Delores was terrified of."

The absolute absurdity of the situation hit all of us at once. I was lying naked between my little sister and her gorgeous, fiercely loyal brunette best friend. The logistics were perfectly aligned for Delores's ultimate nightmare.

"We *could*," I noted, my processor recognizing the hilarious biological variable. "But I think my muscles are too exhausted to even lift my arm right now."

"Same," Kristin laughed softly, resting her head heavy against the pillow right next to mine. "The sentinel is officially off-duty. No adult activities tonight. Just spiteful, glorious sleep."

"Good," Rachel murmured happily, her breathing already starting to slow as she buried her face into the crook of my neck. "Delores is a joke. We won."

We laid there in the dark, the laughter slowly fading into the heavy, comfortable silence of the pack. I closed my eyes, surrounded by the physical warmth of the women who had saved my life, and for the first time in two years, I drifted to sleep without a single trace of fear.

# Book 16: AWE Monday Night War

## Chapter 1: The Portland Offensive (Monday, November 20, 2006)

### Part 1: The Morning Deployment – 6:00 AM (PST)

The sharp, rhythmic electronic beeping of the hotel alarm clock shattered the heavy, insulated silence of the third-floor room.

My processor booted up slowly, pulling me out of the deepest, most restorative sleep I had experienced in two years. I didn't wake up with a violently racing heart or the sudden, terrified urge to scan the room for threats. I just blinked against the dim, early-morning light filtering through the edges of the blackout curtains, my brain immediately registering the absolute, unshakeable physical geometry of my perimeter.

I was still pinned perfectly in the center of the plush queen bed. On my right, Rachel was completely tangled in the heavy white hotel comforter, her bare leg still securely hooked over my calf, her arm draped heavily across my chest. On my left, Kristin let out a soft, sleepy groan. She shifted her weight, pressing her bare shoulder flush against mine, and reached an arm out from under the blankets to blindly swat at the alarm clock on the nightstand.

Her hand found the snooze button, plunging the room back into quiet.

"Morning, Bear," Rachel rasped softly, her dark eyes cracking open.

She didn't move her arm. She just looked across my chest at Kristin, who was blinking sleepily, and then back down at me. A slow, highly amused smirk spread across Rachel's face as she realized the physical reality of our sleeping arrangement hadn't changed by a single inch since we turned the lights out last night.

"Delores is still screaming somewhere," Rachel whispered, her voice dancing with vindictive joy.

I let out a low, rumbling chuckle that vibrated against Rachel's arm. It was true. If Delores could somehow see this room, her mind would completely shatter. But as I lay there, feeling the soft, grounding warmth of Kristin resting comfortably against my left side, my analytical processor ran the logic.

Delores had absolutely no right to scream. She had voluntarily surrendered her territory. She was the one who had screamed at me, packed my bags, and violently thrown me out into the freezing Kent rain at four in the morning. She had broken up with me. She had literally thrown me away like garbage.

So what if I had found someone new? What if I was sharing a bed, completely stripped of armor, with a gorgeous, fiercely loyal twenty-year-old woman who actually protected me? It was none of her business anymore. Delores’s sick, twisted rules were dead.

Kristin felt the vibration of my laugh. She didn't pull away.

"Let her scream," Kristin mumbled playfully from my left. She nestled closer, resting her chin comfortably on my bare shoulder and closing her eyes again. "I'm comfortable. And she's a thousand miles away."

"Alright, strike the perimeter," Jessica’s voice suddenly cut through the darkness from the other bed, followed by the soft click of the bedside lamp clicking on. "It is 6:00 AM. Transit math requires us to be dressed and fully mobilized before the afternoon crowd hits the arena."

The pack moved with practiced, frictionless efficiency. We threw back the heavy comforter.

The zero-privacy baseline held absolute. There was no hesitation, no retreating to the bathroom, and absolutely no shame. Rachel, Kristin, and I stood up in the open space between the two queen beds, completely exposed in the soft lamplight, and began pulling clean clothes from our duffel bags.

For the second day in a row, my threat-detection system remained entirely dormant. The ghosts of Kent were gone. Kristin standing there, treating my physical form with total, unbothered normalcy, was incredibly medicinal. It proved to my trauma-wired brain that the female body was not a weapon.

I pulled on my thick denim jeans and a fresh, dark t-shirt, maintaining the unbroken visual tether with Rachel as she pulled her own signature red shirt over her head. We verified each other's presence, anchoring our nervous systems for the day ahead.

Once we were dressed, Rachel immediately dragged one of the heavy upholstered hotel chairs over to the polished mahogany desk. She sat down, pulling her knees to her chest, and patted the empty chair right next to her.

"You have time to build," Rachel reminded me.

I smiled, my processor buzzing with anticipation. I walked over to the desk and placed my hand on the cool, brushed-aluminum lid of the ten-pound, piano-black machine. In my head, the terminology had officially shifted permanently. It wasn't just a command center anymore. It was the **Extraction Rig**. It was the physical, humming vehicle of my liberation.

I pressed the silver power button. The dual 7200 RPM hard drives whined to life with a satisfying, mechanical rev, and the 17-inch screen illuminated the dark wood of the hotel desk.

I booted *The Denizens 2*, enduring the loud, aggressive SecuROM disc-check as the optical drive spun to maximum RPM.

Once the high-definition digital neighborhood loaded, I pulled up the hidden developer console. *bool_prop edit_citizen true*.

I clicked on the blank, default male avatar waiting in the family bin and initiated the *Force Create-A-Character* override. The screen transitioned to the blue-lit pedestal room, and I went to work.

Rachel immediately hooked her left arm securely around my waist. She leaned her entire body weight flush against my right side, resting her chin heavily on my shoulder so she could watch the screen perfectly.

With her physical pressure grounding me, I used the weighted optical mouse to meticulously sculpt the Bear. I adjusted the jawline and the height. But more importantly, I adjusted the posture. I deliberately bypassed the hunched, defensive slump that Delores had beaten into me over the last two years. I used the game's sliders to give the digital avatar the upright, confident stance of a survivor. I dressed him in a dark, comfortable flannel, perfectly mirroring the heavy armor I had worn on the train ride yesterday.

"There he is," Rachel whispered, her arm tightening around my ribs in a heavy, affectionate squeeze.

I moved my left hand over the keyboard, resting my finger above the 'C' key. Using the mouse, I rotated the digital Bear so he was standing perfectly shoulder-to-shoulder with the digital Bug I had built the night before.

*Click-whir.*

The simulated camera shutter echoed from the premium speakers, capturing the high-resolution screenshot and saving it directly to the Extraction Rig's hard drive. The visual documentation for the *Vertex Creative Studio* lore document was complete. Our digital sanctuary was officially populated, and we were standing tall.

I saved the game and powered down the Extraction Rig, the heavy cooling fans slowly spinning to a halt. My processor felt incredibly light. The digital armor was built. The physical perimeter was secure.

I leaned back in the heavy upholstered chair, looking across the room at my older sister, who was already reviewing transit routes on her corporate laptop.

"So," I said, a massive, unburdened grin breaking across my face. "What time do the arena doors open for AWE Monday Night WAR?"

### Part 2: The Logic of the Ban – 6:45 AM (PST)

At exactly 6:45 AM, a sharp, authoritative knock echoed against the heavy wooden door of our third-floor room.

Kristin, fully embracing her role as the pack’s designated sentinel, didn't hesitate. She marched to the threshold, checked the peephole, and cracked the door just enough to slide the massive, silver-covered room service cart inside. She didn't let the hotel staff cross the boundary. She swiftly signed the receipt, handed the employee a cash tip, and locked the deadbolt with a heavy, satisfying *clack*.

The civilian world was sealed back out in the hallway.

We wheeled the cart to the center of the room, distributing the heavy ceramic plates across the desk and the small table. Jessica took her scrambled eggs and coffee, Kristin claimed her massive Belgian waffle, and Rachel and I sat shoulder-to-shoulder on the edge of the mattress with our identical stacks of buttermilk pancakes, crispy bacon, and cold apple juice.

"I still can't believe the timing on this," I said, pouring a heavy layer of maple syrup over my pancakes. "We get front-row tickets to a live television broadcast, and it just happens to be the exact week Marcus Sterling decides to permanently ban all arena pyrotechnics. If they were setting off those massive concussion mortars and flash pots tonight, our perimeter would be completely shattered."

Rachel took a bite of her bacon. She looked over at me, her dark eyes sparkling with a bright, knowing amusement.

"Sterling didn't ban anything, Bear," Rachel rasped, her voice thick with the absolute, hyper-fixated confidence of someone who knew the hidden mechanics of the AWE universe inside and out.

I paused, my fork hovering over my plate. "What do you mean? We watched the promo on TV last week. He explicitly banned them to cut corporate costs."

"That's just the storyline," Rachel explained, her free hand moving excitedly as she broke down the reality. "I read the dirt sheets and the backstage production forums. The live-event touring department booked the Rose City Center six to eight months ago. Contracts are signed, basic logistics are set. But when you get to the permit phase—usually about four to six weeks out—the pyrotechnician team has to submit a highly detailed 'Pyro Plot' to the local Portland Fire Marshal and the venue's operations manager."

Jessica took a sip of her coffee, her corporate-logistics brain instantly engaging with the explanation. "Let me guess. They detailed every single mortar, gerb fountain, and flash pot, along with the required fallout zones, and the city rejected it."

Rachel nodded vigorously. "A hard denial. In mid-sized or older arenas like this, the Fire Marshal usually kills the pyro for a few specific reasons. Either the arena's HVAC system cannot cycle the smoke out fast enough, which would leave the building dangerously hazy for a two-hour live television broadcast. Or the central scoreboard and lighting grid hang too low, creating a severe fire hazard for vertical spark fountains. Or the local municipality just passed stricter indoor combustion laws that the touring production simply cannot meet."

"So the Fire Marshal killed the pyro," Kristin realized, cutting into her waffle. "Not the General Manager."

"Exactly," Rachel smiled, completely in her element. "Three weeks out, the Lead Pyrotechnician notifies the Head of Production that Portland is a dead zone for fireworks. The Head of Production takes it directly to the Creative Team. They realize that having zero pyro on a live Monday Night television broadcast—especially for stars whose entrances completely rely on the visual and auditory pop—will feel incredibly flat to the viewers at home and the live crowd."

"And they panic?" I asked.

"No, they adapt," Rachel said, her eyes shining with profound respect for the writers. "Two weeks out, the Head Writer looks at the upcoming card and realizes Marcus Sterling is already pushing a 'corporate cost-cutting' angle. They decide to lean completely into the skid. Instead of letting the fans think the company is just being cheap or having technical difficulties, they write the real-world venue restriction into the script."

The absolute brilliance of the maneuver clicked into place in my analytical processor.

"They sent Sterling out there last week to explicitly ban the pyrotechnics," I said, putting the pieces together. "Which achieves two massive goals. First, it perfectly lowers the expectations. The live fans in Portland, and the millions watching at home, are now explicitly expecting a show with no fireworks, so they aren't disappointed when it doesn't happen."

"And," Jessica added, a wicked, appreciative smirk crossing her face, "Sterling takes the blame for something the local government actually did. He absorbs all that real-world frustration and turns it into incredible, localized storyline heat. It perfectly sets up the stakes for whatever the main event is tonight."

"It's the absolute perfect marriage of behind-the-scenes logistics and on-screen storytelling," Rachel finished, taking a happy sip of her apple juice.

I looked at my little sister, overwhelmed by a deep, protective wave of pride. She wasn't just a fan watching a television show; she was a structural engineer analyzing the architecture of a fictional universe. Knowing the logical, real-world permit laws behind the pyrotechnic ban completely stripped away the unpredictability of the arena. It wasn't a chaotic, terrifying environment anymore. It was a highly controlled, permitted, and regulated production.

But as my processor ran the numbers, a massive logistical discrepancy suddenly halted my train of thought.

I lowered my fork and looked across the room at my older sister.

"Wait a minute," I said, my brow furrowing. "Jess... you bought us front-row Ringsider tickets for the *No Quarter* Pay-Per-View in Seattle this Sunday."

"I did," Jessica confirmed casually, taking another sip of coffee.

"Then how did we get *these* tickets?" I asked, gesturing vaguely toward the window and the Rose City Center across the street. "Portland is a massive detour from Renton. And AWE Ringsider tickets are astronomically expensive. You couldn't have afforded to buy front-row seats for a live broadcast *and* a Sunday Pay-Per-View in the same week."

Jessica just smiled, nodding her head toward Rachel. "Ask the VIP."

I looked back at Rachel. She was picking at a piece of bacon, a complicated mix of emotions washing over her face.

"Jessica didn't buy these," Rachel rasped quietly. "Dad won them."

My processor stalled. "Dad?"

"Two weeks ago," Rachel explained, staring down at her plate. "He was driving across the Interstate Bridge into Portland for work. He was listening to the local Classic Rock FM station, and they announced a 'Monday Night WAR VIP Front Row Giveaway.' He dialed the 1-800 request line and happened to be Caller 10. He won the four-pack."

"And he just gave them to you?" I asked, highly skeptical. Tom Ragsdale was notoriously manipulative. He was currently terrified of Jessica's financial guillotine regarding the car she was paying for, and he was completely estranged from us. Handing Rachel a massive VIP package for her ultimate hyper-fixation felt exactly like a trap designed to buy his way back into our good graces.

"He wanted to come," Rachel admitted, her shoulders tensing slightly at the thought. "He genuinely loves wrestling. He called me right after he won and told me we were going. I... I said yes, but I was terrified of having to sit next to him for three hours."

Kristin reached across the mattress and gently squeezed Rachel's knee in a show of quiet support.

"But then his schedule dropped," Rachel continued, her posture relaxing. "His boss at the self-storage facility wouldn't give him the night off. He's locked into the closing shift tonight. He tried to get out of it, but he couldn't. So, he called the radio station and legally transferred the entire VIP package into my name at Will Call. The tickets are one hundred percent mine. He's not coming."

I let out a long breath, the phantom tension of a Tom Ragsdale encounter leaving the room. But as I looked at the math of the situation, a profound realization washed over me.

"A four-pack of tickets," I murmured.

"Yeah," Rachel nodded softly. "I claimed one for me, one for Jess, and one for Kristin. But for over a week, I was just holding onto this vacant fourth ticket. I didn't want to invite a stranger. I just... left it empty."

The geography of the universe suddenly aligned with absolute, breathtaking perfection.

Rachel had been holding a single, empty front-row ticket to her sanctuary for eleven days. And at 4:00 AM on Friday morning, Delores had violently evicted me, initiating the extraction that sent me directly to Washington State.

The fourth ticket was never a spare. It was never an extra. It was a holding pattern.

I looked at my Bug, my heart swelling with a fierce, unbreakable warmth. I reached over, wrapping my arm securely around her shoulders and pulling her into a tight side-hug.

"It's a good thing you kept it empty," I whispered.

Rachel leaned her head heavily against my shoulder, a bright, unburdened smile finally breaking across her face. "It was always your seat, Bear."

I held my Bug close, the profound realization of the fourth ticket settling warmly into my chest. But as my analytical processor finished computing the timeline of Tom Ragsdale’s radio contest, it suddenly collided with a completely separate logistical discrepancy.

The math didn't add up.

I slowly pulled back from Rachel, my brow furrowing in deep confusion. I looked across the room at my older sister, who had gone back to quietly typing on her corporate laptop.

"Wait," I said, my voice dropping as my brain tried to parse the variables. "My processor just hit a massive snag."

Jessica stopped typing, peering over the top of her glasses. "What's the snag?"

"If Dad won the Portland tickets two weeks ago..." I started, pointing a finger in the air to track the timeline. "That means *you* specifically bought the four front-row Ringsider tickets for the *No Quarter* Pay-Per-View in Seattle. And knowing how fast AWE Pay-Per-Views sell out, you had to have bought those tickets months ago."

Jessica simply offered a calm, level nod. "The day they went on sale."

"But," I pressed, the gap in the logic widening, "months ago, I was trapped in Virginia with Delores. Even a week ago, I was stuck on a Pathfinder bus in the middle of nowhere. As far as you knew, I was three thousand miles away, and I wasn't allowed to leave her sight." I looked around the room at the pack. "Why did you buy four tickets?"

The hotel room fell completely silent. Rachel squeezed my hand. Kristin closed her paperback novel, resting it on the mattress and giving Jessica a quiet, knowing look.

Jessica didn't look away. She slowly closed the lid of her laptop, folding her hands neatly on top of the brushed aluminum chassis. The corporate-manager persona dropped entirely, replaced by the fierce, protective intensity of an older sister who had been playing a high-stakes chess game in the dark.

"Because I knew you weren't going to class," Jessica stated, her voice quiet but carrying the weight of absolute certainty.

I blinked, completely caught off guard. "What?"

"I check in on things, Michael. I have my ways," Jessica explained, her tone softening. "I knew you had completely stopped attending your courses at the Community College of Hampton Roads. The data was obvious. Your perimeter was shrinking. You were being isolated, cut off from your education, your family, and your life. Delores was suffocating you."

A cold phantom chill ran down my spine at the mention of the college, a dark reminder of the void Delores had deliberately engineered.

"The midnight rescue in Kent on Thursday?" Jessica continued, shaking her head. "That was never the original plan. That was a catastrophic emergency response because Delores forced the timeline. The original plan was a highly calculated extraction. I had a blueprint."

"What was the blueprint?" I asked, my voice barely above a whisper.

"The Golden Carrot," Jessica smiled, a fierce, tactical light shining behind her glasses. "I knew that if I just asked you to leave her, your threat-detection system would panic. Delores had you too heavily conditioned. I had to offer you something so massive, so logically undeniable, that it would short-circuit her control."

She pointed to Rachel, and then to herself.

"I bought the four *No Quarter* tickets the day they went on sale. The fourth seat was always yours. My plan was to call you in late October. I was going to tell you that Rachel and I had front-row seats to the biggest AWE event of the year, and that Rachel desperately needed her Bear sitting next to her."

Jessica paused, glancing affectionately at our little sister. "To be perfectly honest, if I could have only afforded two tickets, they would have been for you and Rachel. Because your sister absolutely despised Delores, and she wanted you out of that toxic relationship more than anything in the world."

Rachel nodded fiercely beside me, her dark eyes flashing with absolute conviction.

"I had thousands of dollars set aside in a specific deployment fund," Jessica revealed, laying out the sheer financial magnitude of her logistics. "I was prepared to pay through the absolute nose for a last-minute, direct, First-Class flight from ORF to SEA. I was going to send you the itinerary with one strict, non-negotiable condition: *The ticket is for Michael. Only Michael. Delores is not invited.*"

My processor stalled. The sheer audacity of the plan—the amount of corporate logistics and financial firepower my sister was preparing to drop on my abuser—was staggering.

"The choice would have been entirely yours," Jessica said softly. "Get on the plane, fly First-Class across the country, sit in the front row with your sister, and come back to the family. Or stay in the squalor."

"And the Extraction Rig?" I asked, looking down at the massive, ten-pound piano-black laptop resting quietly on the desk.

"That was Phase Two," Jessica grinned. "If I got you out here for the Pay-Per-View in November, I knew you'd be terrified of the fallout of going back. So, the laptop was going to be your Christmas present. The ultimate anchor. A brand-new, top-of-the-line digital sanctuary, given to you with zero strings attached, to permanently break her conditioning and keep you here in Washington for good."

I sat completely frozen on the edge of the mattress.

For two years, Delores had hammered into my brain that I was worthless. She convinced me that my family didn't care about me, that they were a threat, and that I had absolutely no value outside of serving her.

But sitting in this hotel room, the truth completely incinerated her lies.

Jessica hadn't forgotten me. She hadn't given up. While I was isolated in a decaying house on the East Coast, my older sister was actively building a war chest. She was plotting transit routes, buying front-row tickets, and securing bleeding-edge technology, engineering a flawless, impenetrable safety net to catch me the second I was ready to jump.

The fact that Delores had dragged me to Seattle on a miserable Pathfinder bus and thrown me out into the rain hadn't ruined Jessica's plan. It had just bypassed the flight logistics and forced the pack to execute the endgame a month early.

I looked at Jessica, my vision suddenly blurring with a thick, stinging heat. I didn't have the words to compute the magnitude of her loyalty.

I stood up from the mattress, crossed the short distance to the desk, and wrapped my arms tightly around my older sister's shoulders. I buried my face into her neck, letting out a long, shuddering breath. Jessica immediately hugged me back, her hands gripping my heavy flannel shirt.

"I've always got your back, Michael," Jessica whispered fiercely into my ear. "Always."

I pulled back, wiping my eyes with the sleeve of my shirt, and offered her a massive, watery smile.

"Well," I said, my voice thick with emotion as I looked around the fortress we had built. "The Golden Carrot worked. I'm right here."

### Part 3: The Holding Pattern – 10:00 AM (PST)

I said the words, but the sheer, crushing weight of what Jessica had just revealed finally broke through the last remaining walls of my defense.

For two years, my body had been locked in a permanent, rigid state of survival. I wasn't allowed to cry in Kent. If I ever showed a single ounce of emotional weakness, Delores would immediately weaponize it, screaming that I was pathetic or punishing me for making her feel bad. I had swallowed every tear, every ounce of terror, until my chest felt like it was packed with wet cement.

But sitting in this hotel room, realizing that my older sister had been willing to empty her bank accounts and drop thousands of dollars just to give me a single chance to escape... the wet cement completely shattered.

A low, guttural rumble ripped from the bottom of my lungs.

My legs gave out. I collapsed heavily back into the upholstered desk chair I had just been sitting in. I buried my face in my hands, and the crying hit me like a physical wave. It was loud. It was heavy. It was the ugly, breathless, earth-shattering sobbing of a hostage who finally realizes the war is actually over.

Instantly, the perimeter closed around me.

Rachel was there in a microsecond, dropping to her knees beside the chair and wrapping her arms fiercely around my waist, burying her face into my ribs. Jessica wheeled her chair in tight, her hands gripping my forearm with a firm, unshakeable pressure.

But then, the geometry of the room shifted.

Kristin dragged the second upholstered chair across the carpet, planting it directly next to mine. She didn't hesitate. She reached out, wrapped her arms around my shaking shoulders, and physically pulled my upper body against hers.

Because of her height—a sturdy, protective five-foot-nine—the angle was flawless. My face fell perfectly against her chest and shoulder. I didn't pull away. Instead, acting on a desperate, blind instinct, I leaned all of my weight into her. I gripped the fabric of her shirt, burying my face into the soft, secure curve of her neck as I sobbed.

She just held me, wrapping her arms around my back and applying a heavy, deep, crushing pressure that instantly started to ground my racing heart.

As I stayed buried against her, a sudden, blinding realization cut through the thick emotional fog in my brain.

Kristin wasn't my sister. She wasn't my cousin. She shared absolutely no blood with me.

Delores had spent two years projecting her sick, twisted jealousy onto my family, trying to isolate me from the sisters who loved me. But Delores’s ultimate, terrifying nightmare was me finding *another* woman. If Delores could somehow see me right now, completely enveloped in the arms of a gorgeous, taller, fiercely protective brunette... she wouldn't just be jealous. She would be completely, utterly defeated. She would realize her territory was permanently gone. She would have to leave me alone.

The logic locked into place. Kristin wasn't just offering comfort; she was a living, breathing shield against the monster.

Through my tears, I felt Rachel shift against my ribs. I couldn't see my sister's face, but I felt her hand reach up and gently pat Kristin's knee. I heard Jessica let out a soft, knowing exhale. They saw exactly what I was doing. They watched me completely bypass my terror of being touched by a non-relative, and they instantly understood the silent math. They knew why I was clinging to the roommate. It was the ultimate barricade.

The heavy, agonizing grief pouring out of my lungs slowly began to transform. The tears were still falling rapidly, but the suffocating darkness was gone. It was just pure, overwhelming, blinding joy. My brain was desperately trying to categorize the warmth radiating from Kristin, rewriting two years of horrific conditioning in a matter of minutes. She was safe. She was an absolute sanctuary.

Eventually, the heavy sobbing slowed to quiet, shuddering breaths.

I sat there, my face still pressed firmly against Kristin's shoulder. My throat was completely locked tight. The invisible, familiar wall had slammed down over my vocal cords, leaving me entirely without the ability to speak. But I didn't want to find my voice anyway. And I absolutely did not want to let go of Kristin.

She didn't make me. She just kept her arms wrapped securely around my back, resting her chin softly on top of my head while Rachel stayed anchored to my side. We stayed like that until the storm completely passed.

With the breakfast cart cleared and my tears finally dried, we settled into a long, restorative holding pattern.

The live broadcast didn't start until 6:00 PM, and Will Call didn't open until mid-afternoon. We had hours of dead time to kill, and absolutely zero intention of leaving the fortress to do it. The civilian world outside the Crown Regency was cold, damp, and filling up with thousands of unpredictable wrestling fans. We stayed off the grid.

Jessica sat at the polished mahogany desk, her corporate laptop open as she quietly mapped out the CTS transit schedules for tomorrow's excursion to Vancouver. Kristin eventually moved to the far bed, lying on her stomach and reading a paperback novel, completely relaxed in our shared space.

Rachel and I spent the rest of the morning anchored to the mattress nearest the window. I had my heavy, noise-isolating headphones resting around my neck, while Rachel had her own pair sitting safely in her lap. We didn't need to speak; the silence between us was loud and perfectly understood. I pulled a thick notebook from my duffel bag and began sketching out physical diagrams of the headless CMS architecture I wanted to build for my music catalog, occasionally pausing to point out the routing logic to Rachel. She would nod, leaning her heavy, comforting weight against my shoulder.

By 1:00 PM, the anticipation in the room began to physically hum.

Rachel's leg started bouncing in a rapid, rhythmic drumbeat against the mattress. She packed her heavy isolation headphones into her small bag, double-checked her wallet for her Washington State ID, and stood up from the bed.

"Armor up," Jessica announced, snapping her laptop shut and sliding it into her messenger bag.

We moved as a single, perfectly synchronized unit. I pulled on my thick denim jeans, my heavy work boots, and my warm winter coat. Rachel slipped into her cargo pants and a soft hoodie, securing her belongings. We didn't need to discuss the formation; the physical geometry was already permanently locked into our muscle memory.

At 2:15 PM, we stepped out of the hotel room.

### Part 4: The Plaza Approach – 2:20 PM (PST)

We took the elevator down to the lobby and pushed through the heavy revolving glass doors of the Crown Regency.

The freezing, damp Pacific Northwest air hit our faces immediately. For my overloaded processor, the sharp bite of the November wind was the exact physical reset I needed. It shocked my system, clearing the last lingering traces of the heavy emotional fog that had kept me completely silent since my meltdown that morning.

The logistical brilliance of Jessica's hotel choice paid off the second our boots hit the pavement. We didn't have to navigate a crowded transit platform or squeeze onto a packed CTS train to get to the venue. We simply walked out the front doors and crossed the street.

The Rose City Center was massive, taking up an entire city block. As we approached the sprawling concrete plaza, the sheer scale of the operation became apparent. Massive AWE production trucks—painted with high-gloss graphics of Silas Thorne, Roxy Dane, and Jaxson Vance—were parked near the loading docks. The low, heavy rumble of diesel generators vibrated through the soles of our boots. Thick, black broadcast cables snaked across the concrete, and event staff in bright yellow jackets were already setting up the heavy steel barricades to corral the impending crowds.

Suddenly, Rachel's hand shot out. Her fingers gripped the thick sleeve of my winter coat like a vice.

I immediately stopped walking, allowing her to press her shoulder flush against my bicep. I looked down at her. Her eyes were wide, darting frantically between the massive production trucks, the arena signage, and the growing crowd of early-arrival fans. She was taking in the massive, overwhelming reality of her ultimate hyperfixation existing in physical space right in front of her.

Her threat-detection system wasn't spiking out of fear; it was simply paralyzed by the sheer, unadulterated magnitude of the Monday Night WAR.

My analytical brain saw the shutdown happening, and it triggered the absolute heaviest, most fundamental subroutine in my programming: *Protect the Bug.*

I swallowed hard, pushing against the heavy, invisible wall that had locked my vocal cords tight for the last four hours. I needed my voice. She needed her anchor.

"I've got you, Bug," I murmured.

The words came out as a quiet, raspy whisper, but the physical vibration of my chest against her shoulder made her blink. She looked up at me, her breathing shallow.

I wrapped my arm securely around her shoulders, pulling her flush against my side, and forced myself to speak again, pushing a little harder against the block.

"Look at the cables, Rachel," I said, my voice gaining a fraction more volume, stepping into the steady, mechanical cadence that always grounded her. "Look at the logistics. It's exactly what you told us at breakfast. It's not chaos. It's a highly controlled broadcast."

Rachel followed my pointing finger. She looked at the heavy black cables, neatly taped down to the concrete, routing perfectly from the production trucks into the massive loading bays. Her rapid breathing hitched, then slowly began to deepen as her brain latched onto the recognizable, underlying structure of the event.

"See?" I continued, the invisible wall in my throat finally shattering completely. The act of protecting her had acted as the ultimate system override. My voice dropped into its full, resonant, unwavering power. "It's all mapped out. Just like your production sheets. We are walking into a controlled environment, and our perimeter is completely solid."

Rachel let out a long, shuddering exhale. Her death grip on my winter coat relaxed into a steady, comfortable hold. She offered a small, grateful nod, her eyes shining with pure excitement rather than overload.

Jessica, who had paused her manual wheelchair to give us the space to regulate, offered a proud, subtle smirk. She wheeled forward smoothly over the wet concrete, retaking the point position to carve a path through the plaza. Kristin fell in line a half-step behind Rachel. Standing at a sturdy five-foot-nine, Kristin naturally eclipsed the rest of us, her broader frame effortlessly guarding her best friend's blind spot from the unpredictable civilian foot traffic.

We walked as a single, impenetrable unit past the towering, multi-story glass windows of the arena's main atrium, making our way directly toward the illuminated box office windows.

My vocal cords were fully online. My spine was straight. The Bear was fully awake. The trap Tom Ragsdale had inadvertently set for his daughter was about to be effortlessly dismantled, because Rachel wasn't walking up to that glass window alone.

## Chapter 2: Arrival at Rose City Center (Monday, November 20, 2006)

### Part 1: Will Call – 2:30 PM (PST)

The massive glass and steel facade of the Rose City Center loomed over us against the overcast, chilly Portland sky.

We made our way across the sprawling concrete plaza toward the box office. Jessica rolled smoothly beside us in her manual wheelchair, her breath visible in the damp November air. Kristin walked a half-step behind Rachel, keeping a protective, familiar proximity to her best friend, while I carried the collective anxiety of the group squarely on my shoulders.

When we reached the Will Call windows, the area was still relatively quiet. The doors wouldn't officially open to the 14,000 screaming fans for another two hours.

I stepped back, allowing Rachel to approach the glass.

When Tom had called the radio station to transfer the tickets out of his name, he had specifically designated Rachel as the primary pickup contact. It was a calculated, heavy move. I couldn't help but feel a dark twist in my gut thinking about it. Dad had been absolutely cruel to Rachel back in 2001, dismissing her and treating her neurodivergence like a burden. But now, five years later, he was forcing her to be the one to physically sign for his grand, expensive "olive branch." It felt like a trap wrapped in a gift box, but for tonight, we were playing along.

Rachel stepped up to the window. Due to her severe situational mutism, especially in unfamiliar public spaces, she didn't say a word. She just took a deep breath, pulled her Washington State ID from her wallet, and slid it quietly through the metal slot under the thick glass.

The box office attendant smiled, taking the ID and turning to a filing cabinet. A moment later, he returned, holding a thick, premium envelope stamped with the metallic AWE Monday Night WAR logo.

He slid the envelope, along with a clipboard, back through the slot. Rachel picked up the pen with a slightly trembling hand and signed her name on the release line.

"Alright, Rachel, you are all set," the attendant said through the speaker system. "Inside that envelope are your four front-row ringside tickets. You also have a VIP Merchandise Voucher. Because you won the premium radio package, I actually have two options for how you can redeem your gear today."

Rachel looked back at me, her eyes wide with sudden, overwhelming nervousness. The unexpected deviation from the standard script was throwing her processor off, and she was silently asking me to step in and process the logistics. I moved up to the glass, standing shoulder-to-shoulder with her, and firmly wrapped my arm around her. I applied a steady, deep pressure against her shoulder, giving her a physical anchor to help ground her racing nervous system.

"What are the options?" I asked, keeping my voice calm and level.

"Option A is the standard route," the attendant explained. "You can take that voucher to the main concourse right now, pick out your shirts and hats, and get it all out of the way before the crowds show up."

"And Option B?" Jessica asked, wheeling up slightly closer.

The attendant grinned. "Option B is a little more hands-on. AWE television production is actually looking for a small group of VIP fans to be extras in a pre-taped segment for tonight's broadcast. If you choose Option B, you have to sign a standard television release form right now. But in exchange, you get to be on national television, and your prize packs will be handed to you directly by one of the wrestlers on camera."

The entire group froze.

I turned my head. Kristin was already smiling, her eyes dancing with excitement. Jessica leaned back in her wheelchair, a knowing smirk crossing her face.

But I looked directly at Rachel. I could feel her trembling slightly beneath my arm, but it wasn't from anxiety anymore. AWE Monday Night WAR wasn't just a television show to her; it was her absolute favorite hyperfixation in the world. The sheer magnitude of what was just offered—the chance to not only meet the wrestlers but to actually exist inside the fictional universe she loved so much—was radiating off of her in waves of pure, unadulterated autistic joy.

She didn't have to speak. Her face said everything.

"We'll take Option B," I told the attendant without a single second of hesitation.

"Excellent choice," he said, sliding four thick, legal release waivers through the slot. "Sign these, and a segment producer will be right out to escort you onto the concourse."

Jessica, Kristin, and I quickly grabbed pens, but Rachel was the first one to finish signing her name. We were officially about to step onto the frontlines of the Monday Night WAR.

### Part 2: The Arsenal – 2:45 PM (PST)

A few minutes after we handed the signed waivers back to the box office attendant, a heavy steel door next to the ticket windows clicked open.

Stepping out onto the concrete plaza was a man who looked like he was carved out of granite. He wasn't exceptionally tall, but he was built like a fire hydrant, carrying a clipboard and walking with a stiff, permanent limp—the undeniable toll of a career spent sacrificing his body in the ring. He wore a black AWE production polo and a headset resting around his neck, projecting an aura of absolute, ice-cold professionalism.

To the casual fan, he was just a backstage producer. But to anyone who actually understood the mechanics of professional wrestling, he was a living legend.

It was Dave "The Arsenal" Cross. The Man of 1,000 Holds.

Before a severe neck injury forced him into early retirement a few years ago, Dave was the most feared, surgically precise technical grappler on the planet. And more importantly, he was Rachel's absolute favorite wrestler of all time.

I felt Rachel’s entire body go rigid beside me. Her hands shot up to chest height, her fingers fluttering rapidly in a pure, uncontrolled stim of absolute disbelief. She was standing three feet away from her hero.

"Afternoon," Dave said, his voice quiet and gravelly. He didn't have a fake, booming television persona. He was just a serious man doing a serious job. "I'm Dave, one of the segment producers. You're our radio contest winners?"

"Yes, sir," I answered, instinctively tightening my arm around Rachel to keep her grounded before she completely floated away. "I'm Michael. This is Kristin, my older sister Jessica, and my younger sister Rachel."

Dave nodded, looking down at the signed waivers on his clipboard to verify the names. He traced a calloused finger down the paper, stopping abruptly. He looked back up, his stoic, intense eyes locking directly onto Rachel.

"Rachel Ragsdale?" Dave asked, his brow furrowing slightly. "From Washington State?"

Rachel, completely paralyzed by situational mutism and awe, could only manage a frantic, wide-eyed nod.

The ice-cold demeanor of "The Arsenal" suddenly melted into a rare, genuine smile. "I'll be damned," he said softly. "I know exactly who you are."

He stepped slightly closer, lowering his clipboard. "You send letters to the Stamford office. You're the one who completely broke down the biomechanics of my inverted Texas Cloverleaf. You actually corrected the broadcast team's commentary on how the fulcrum point works on the lower back."

Rachel's face flushed bright red, and she nodded violently, a massive, silent smile stretching across her face.

"Most people write to tell me I'm their favorite," Dave said, his tone filled with deep, unmistakable respect. "You write to tell me why the mechanics work. Nobody else does that. I kept those letters. Thank you, Rachel. It means a lot to know someone actually watches the footwork."

If Rachel could have spoken, she wouldn't have had the words anyway. The sheer, validating joy of being recognized not just as a fan, but as a student of the craft by her ultimate hero, was a monumental moment. Kristin gently squeezed Rachel's shoulder, beaming with pride for her best friend.

"Alright," Dave said, smoothly shifting back into producer mode, though his eyes were still warm. "Let's get you inside. We're taping a segment on the main concourse in about twenty minutes. We've got 'Sly' Diego Chavez out there."

Dave held the heavy door open for us, making sure Jessica had plenty of clearance for her wheelchair as we crossed the threshold into the quiet, cavernous arena.

"Here is the setup," Dave explained as we walked down the empty, polished concrete hallways. "In the storyline, Diego just picked the pocket of 'The Tycoon' Preston Sinclair backstage. He stole Sinclair's platinum money clip, which is loaded with hundred-dollar bills. Diego is going to come out to the main merchandise stand, cut a promo, and use Sinclair's stolen cash to buy out the merch stand for the fans."  

We turned a corner, and the massive, brightly lit AWE merchandise stand came into view. A small camera crew was busy setting up lighting rigs and taping cables to the floor.

"Since you four are our VIPs, you are playing our lucky fans," Dave said, stopping us just out of frame of the cameras. "Before we roll, I need you to look at the wall and tell the merch staff exactly which shirts, hats, and autographed photos you want for your prize packs. We'll bag the photos and belts for you to hold onto, but Diego is going to physically toss your hats and shirts to you on camera. When he throws the gear, I just need you to go crazy. Make it look like the best day of your life."  

He looked back at Rachel and offered a small wink. "Given the letters you write, I don't think you'll have to act too hard."

### Part 3: The Arsenal's Promise – 3:15 PM (PST)

The main merchandise stand was a towering, brightly lit wall of black cotton, neon graphics, and glossy photographs. Even completely empty of the usual thousands of pushing, shoving fans, it was an overwhelming display of AWE spectacle.

An arena vendor was waiting for us behind the massive tables, looking slightly bored until Dave walked up and tapped the counter.

"Alright, folks," Dave said, gesturing to the wall. "As contest winners, you each get the full VIP spread. That means one hat, one t-shirt, one of those giant foam fingers, a mini replica championship belt, and an autographed eight-by-ten photo of any wrestler on the active roster."

"And don't forget the chairs," the vendor chimed in, pointing toward the arena floor. "Your tickets are Row 1. Those padded Ringsider folding chairs you're sitting in tonight? You get to fold those up and take them home after the main event."

Jessica let out a low whistle. "We're going to need an extra set of hands just to get all this onto the train tomorrow morning."

"We'll manage," I said, grinning.

Kristin immediately stepped up to the counter, her eyes scanning the wall of apparel. She confidently pointed out a studded-graphic Roxy "The Riot" Dane t-shirt and an autographed photo of the kickboxer. Jessica, ever the pragmatist, opted for a classic, understated black AWE logo hoodie and a glossy photo of "Sly" Diego Chavez.

I looked over at Rachel. She was standing perfectly still, her eyes darting frantically across the dozens of t-shirts pinned to the massive display grid. She looked down at the organized binders of autographed photos on the table, then back up to the wall. Her hands, which had been resting happily at her sides, slowly drifted up to her chest, her fingers curling inward.

I knew that posture. It was the physical manifestation of a rapidly crashing expectation.

I stepped closer to her, keeping my voice low. "Rachel? What's wrong? You have the pick of the entire litter."

She didn't speak. She just looked at me, her eyes welling up with tears, and gave her head a tiny, frustrated shake. She pointed a trembling finger toward the wall, and then made a subtle, sweeping gesture.

It took me a second to translate, but when I looked at the wall, it clicked. Rachel didn't want a Silas Thorne shirt. She didn't want a Jaxson Vance glossy.

She wanted Dave "The Arsenal" Cross gear. And because Dave had been retired from in-ring competition for years, his merchandise was no longer carried on the live touring trucks.

Rachel wasn't having a meltdown, but the profound sadness of standing right next to her absolute hero and not being able to represent him was threatening to push her into a sensory shutdown. She had built this exact moment up in her head, and the missing variable was causing a neurological misfire.

Before I could figure out how to pivot her attention, Dave stepped in. He had been watching the exchange silently.

"Hey," Dave said softly, his gravelly voice cutting through the quiet hum of the concourse. He stepped right up to the counter, putting himself at eye level with Rachel. "You looking for the old black-and-silver 'Arsenal' skull shirt?"

Rachel looked at him and gave a small, heartbreaking nod, a single tear escaping down her cheek.

Dave offered a warm, understanding smile. "I'm sorry, Rachel. I haven't had a shirt on that wall in three years. They only carry the active guys on the trucks now."

Rachel looked down at her shoes. I prepared to wrap my arm around her to start the grounding process, but Dave held up a hand, stopping me.

"But," Dave continued, leaning casually against the merch table. "I happen to know for a fact that the Stamford warehouse still has a few boxes of deadstock Arsenal gear sitting on a pallet in the back. And as a segment producer, I have the direct phone number to the warehouse manager."

Rachel's head snapped up.

"If you let Michael write down your shirt size and home address for me," Dave said, pulling a pen out of his pocket. "I will personally pay for an Arsenal t-shirt and hat, and I will have them overnight shipped directly to your house in Washington. Deal?"

The heavy, suffocating sadness instantly evaporated from Rachel's face, replaced by a look of absolute, starry-eyed wonder. The fact that Dave Cross—the Man of 1,000 Holds—was going to personally mail her his gear was infinitely better than buying it off a generic arena wall.

Rachel nodded enthusiastically, her hands flapping in a quick, happy burst of restored energy. A massive meltdown had just been flawlessly averted by the simple, observant kindness of a retired wrestler.

"Awesome," Dave said, handing me his notebook to jot down the Renton address. "In the meantime, you still have a free prize pack to claim right now. What's it going to be to hold you over?"

With her processor fully reset and the world making sense again, Rachel happily stepped up to the counter. She pointed to a classic Monday Night WAR logo shirt and selected an eight-by-ten glossy of Jaxson "The Outlaw" Vance to support the hometown Portland hero. I quickly followed suit, grabbing a Resistance faction shirt and a mini replica of the World Heavyweight Championship.

Within minutes, the vendor had our photos and mini belts safely bagged and set aside, leaving only the four t-shirts, four hats, and four giant foam fingers piled neatly on the counter.

"Alright, production crew is ready!" Dave called out, clipping his radio back onto his belt. "Let's make some television!"

### Part 4: The Tycoon, The Thief, and the Foam Finger – 3:45 PM (PST)

"Alright, everyone to your marks!" Dave Cross ordered, his voice echoing slightly in the vast, empty concourse. He pointed us toward the front of the merchandise table. "Stay loose. When Diego throws the money, just go crazy."

An AWE marketing rep crouched just out of the camera's frame, holding the specific hats, shirts, and the giant foam finger we had just picked out.

"Cameras speed. And... action!"

The heavy double doors down the concourse banged open. "Sly" Diego Chavez burst into the frame, jogging backward and flashing his signature, million-dollar smirk. He was waving a platinum money clip stuffed with thick, green hundred-dollar bills.

"Chavez!" a voice roared from the hallway.

Stomping into the light was "The Tycoon" Preston Sinclair. The billionaire heel was dressed in his pristine white Texan suit, his face contorted in absolute fury. But instead of a microphone, Sinclair was dragging a massive, thick-braided Texas Bull Rope behind him, a heavy iron cowbell clanking violently against the concrete floor.

"You think you can steal from me?!" Sinclair bellowed, winding the thick rope around his wrist. "I'm going to skin you alive this Sunday!"

"You gotta catch me first, Tycoon!" Diego yelled back, seamlessly hyping their upcoming Pay-Per-View bout.

Sinclair lunged forward, swinging the heavy bull rope with terrifying speed. The cowbell whipped through the air, but Diego nimbly dropped to a knee, ducking the strike perfectly. The rope smashed loudly against a concrete pillar instead, echoing through the concourse.

Diego just laughed, hopping effortlessly onto the top of the merchandise table. Sinclair tried to charge, but two AWE security extras rushed into the frame, holding the furious billionaire back.

"Oye, Portland!" Diego shouted, playing to the imaginary television audience and looking down at us. "Let me tell you a little secret about my good friend, Preston Sinclair! He thinks tying me up in a Texas Bull Rope Match this Sunday at No Quarter is gonna keep me down! But the man is incredibly generous! In fact, Señor Sinclair just told me he wants to buy every single person in this line some brand new gear! On him!"

We hit our cue perfectly. Kristin, Jessica, and I started cheering loudly, but Rachel was completely lost in the magic of the moment. She was jumping up and down, clapping her hands in a flurry of uncontainable excitement.

The AWE rep handed Diego our pre-selected merchandise out of frame.

"Give the kid in the front a hat!" Diego shouted, tossing the classic AWE cap directly into my hands.

"Give the lady in the back a foam finger, homes!" Diego yelled, looking right at Rachel. He tossed the giant, blue-and-silver foam prop. Rachel caught it, clutching it to her chest like it was made of solid gold.

Diego turned to the bewildered merchandise cashier, peeling a thick stack of bills off Sinclair's platinum clip and slapping them onto the table. "Keep the change, amigo! The Tycoon is paying for it!"

"Cut! Print that!" Dave called out from behind the monitors. "Great job, everyone. Reset for the backstage shot."

The second the word "Cut" hung in the air, the heavy, fictional tension evaporated. But for Rachel, the sheer weight of what was happening finally crashed down on her all at once.

She was going to be on national television. Dave Cross was personally mailing a shirt to her house. She was holding a prop used by one of her favorite wrestlers. It was entirely too much joy, too much electricity for her system to process at one time.

Rachel's knees buckled. She dropped to the polished concrete floor, pulling her knees to her chest and clutching the foam finger tight, burying her face in it as a wave of intense, frantic tears overtook her. Her free hand began to shake rapidly in a physical release of the overwhelming surge of emotion.

Instantly, the atmosphere shifted.

"Whoa, whoa, back up," Diego Chavez said. The charismatic, loudmouth persona vanished in a microsecond. The man behind the character—a father to a special-needs child in real life—recognized the signs immediately. He knew exactly what he was looking at.

He didn't rush her. He didn't bend down and demand to know if she was okay, which would have only made everything worse. Instead, Diego immediately turned his back to Rachel, holding his arms out wide to create a physical buffer zone between her and the camera crew.

"Give her space, guys," Diego instructed the crew in a calm, authoritative tone. "No cameras. Back it up."

Preston Sinclair, who had been panting and trying to fix his ruined suit, saw the commotion. He took one step toward Rachel out of genuine concern, but Diego caught his eye and gave a subtle, flat-handed gesture. Give her space. Sinclair, knowing about Diego's own family dynamics, nodded once in silent understanding and stepped back to help hold the perimeter.

With the outer perimeter secured by the two wrestlers, I immediately dropped to the floor right beside Rachel.

I didn't try to pull her to her feet or ask her what was wrong. I just wrapped my arms around her and pulled her into a very, very tight bear hug. I locked my arms and squeezed, applying deep, heavy, crushing pressure to her torso and shoulders. It was the only thing that ever grounded her when she got like this—a physical anchor to keep her from floating away into the panic.

Jessica wheeled her chair in close, speaking in a low, rhythmic, familiar cadence about the train ride tomorrow, giving Rachel a steady voice to focus on. Kristin stood tall right behind us, shielding Rachel's back from the rest of the arena.

I held her tightly against me, letting her squeeze the foam finger between us, until I felt her rapid breathing finally start to slow down. The frantic shaking in her hand gradually eased. After a couple of minutes, she wiped her eyes, looked up from the foam finger, and offered a massive, exhausted, but radiantly happy smile.

"You good?" I whispered, finally loosening my grip just a fraction.

She nodded firmly.

Diego looked over his shoulder. Seeing that the storm had passed and Rachel was smiling, he offered a warm, subtle nod of respect—first to Rachel, and then to me. He didn't make a scene or draw further attention to it. He just turned to Dave Cross and gave a thumbs-up.

"Alright, folks, you're clear," Dave said gently, walking over as I finally let go and helped Rachel to her feet. He reached into his pocket and pulled out the thick stack of bills Diego had slapped on the counter. He peeled off four of the realistic-looking \$100 bills and handed one to each of us.

Printed across the top in small letters were the words: For Motion Picture Use Only.

"Souvenirs from the Tycoon," Dave said with a smirk. "Keep 'em safe. And Rachel? Keep an eye on your mailbox next week."

Rachel clutched the prop money and her foam finger, her smile brighter than the arena lights. The main event hadn't even started yet, and it was already the greatest night of her life.

### Part 5: The Descent and the Trust Fall – 4:15 PM (PST)

With Rachel securely grounded and our massive haul of VIP merchandise gathered up, it was time to actually find our seats.

"We need to get you guys down to the floor," Dave Cross told us, checking his watch. "The problem is, the only ADA-compliant elevator that goes from this concourse directly down to the arena floor level is located strictly behind the curtain, in the restricted locker room area. You can't get back there without an escort. Follow me."

For a wrestling fan, taking the restricted freight elevator was a golden ticket. Dave led us past a pair of massive security guards and through a set of heavy double doors. Suddenly, we were in the belly of the beast. We walked past towering stacks of black metal road cases stenciled with the AWE logo, dodging production assistants sprinting with headsets and lighting technicians carrying coils of heavy cabling.

Dave swiped his badge, and the heavy metal doors of the freight elevator slid open. We piled in, the hydraulic motor humming loudly as we descended to the ground level of the Rose City Center.

When the doors opened, Dave led us down a short concrete tunnel that spilled directly out onto the arena floor.

The sheer scale of the building from the floor level was breathtaking. Looking up into the cavernous rafters, surrounded by 14,000 empty seats, the magnitude of Monday Night WAR finally set in. But as we approached the ringside area, the logistical reality of our front-row tickets slammed into us like a brick wall.

"Okay," Jessica said, stopping her manual wheelchair at the edge of the floor mats. "That’s going to be a problem."

She was right. The front-row seats were directly against the heavy steel barricade, dead center with the ring. But to get to them, you had to navigate a minefield of thick, black television broadcast cables duct-taped to the concrete. Even worse, the premium padded folding chairs were zip-tied together to prevent them from moving.

They were jammed so incredibly close to one another that there was zero personal space. When you sat down, you were going to be completely shoulder-to-shoulder, physically touching the person sitting next to you. There was absolutely no room to navigate a wheelchair down the row, and the steel barricade blocked any access from the front.

"Leave the chair here by the tunnel entrance," I told Jessica, stepping up without missing a beat. "I'll carry you."

This was our standard operational protocol. I was the only one in the family with the physical strength and balance to carry my twenty-six-year-old sister. Jessica locked the brakes on her wheelchair, and I crouched down in front of her. She leaned forward, wrapping her arms securely around my neck. I hooked my arms under her legs and hoisted her up into a piggyback hold.

It was a massive display of absolute, unspoken sibling trust. She trusted me to carry her safely over the trip hazards, and I trusted her to hold on tight.

I carefully navigated the tight, awkward gap between the barricade and the first row of chairs, carrying Jessica all the way to the dead center of the section. I gently lowered her into the far-left seat of our block.

We filed in right behind her. Facing the ring, the seating order was locked in: Jessica on the far left, me sitting immediately to her right, Rachel next to me, and Kristin on the far right.

Because we were in the exact center of the long row, random fans would be sitting directly to Jessica's left and Kristin's right. Jessica was completely boxed in. With the chairs zip-tied together and people soon to be crammed into every available inch of space, there was absolutely no way Jessica was getting out of that seat to use the restroom or leave the arena unless I stood up and carried her back out the exact same way we came in. She was entirely dependent on my physical anchor, but she didn't look nervous. She just patted my shoulder and settled into her padded chair.

We were exhausted and running on adrenaline, which meant the inevitable hunger was starting to set in.

"I was hoping to grab a pretzel or something on the way down," Kristin said, looking around the floor. "But the concourse concession stands had their metal grates pulled down."

"They don't open the main food stands until the doors officially open to the public," I explained, pulling my heavy isolation headphones out of my bag and resting them around my neck. "We're going to have to rely on the roaming vendors selling peanuts and sodas once the crowd fills in. Thank God we ate that massive lunch before we got to the arena."

"I'm fine," Rachel said, speaking for the first time since we entered the building. Her voice was quiet, but she was smiling, her hands happily tracing the AWE logo on her giant foam finger.

At exactly 4:30 PM, the arena doors opened, and the Portland crowd began to flood into the building like a tidal wave. The ambient noise level rose from a quiet hum to a chaotic roar as thousands of fans found their seats.

At 5:30 PM, the massive overhead house lights suddenly dimmed, bathing the arena floor in a cool, shadowy blue. The bright, blinding spotlights over the ring snapped on.

The untelevised dark matches were about to begin. The Monday Night WAR had officially arrived.

### Part 6: The Dark Matches – 5:30 PM (PST)

At exactly 5:30 PM, the massive overhead house lights of the Rose City Center completely cut out. A collective, electric roar rippled through the 14,000 fans as the blinding, brilliant white spotlights suspended above the ring snapped on, illuminating the canvas like a pristine stage.

The live television broadcast was still thirty minutes away, but the AWE always used untelevised "dark matches" to whip the live crowd into a frenzy before the cameras officially started rolling.

As a rookie tag team sprinted down the ramp to an upbeat rock track, high-fiving fans along the barricade, a booming, rhythmic voice cut through the ambient noise in our section.

"POPCORN! PEANUTS! ICE COLD BEER! GET YOUR ICE COLD BEER HERE!"

A roving arena vendor was squeezing his way down the narrow aisle behind the second row, carrying a massive, plastic-wrapped tray suspended by a neck strap.

"Hey! Over here!" I called out, twisting around in my zip-tied folding chair and waving an arm to flag him down.

The vendor leaned over the shoulders of the people in the second row, flashing a salesman's grin. "What can I get you, boss? Two tall boys to start the night right?"

"We'll skip the beer," I said with a laugh, gesturing down the row. "My younger sister and her best friend are only twenty anyway. But we are absolutely starving. We're going to need a lot of everything else."

I reached into my wallet and pulled out a stack of actual cash—careful not to mix it up with the Motion Picture Use Only hundreds Dave Cross had just given us.

Because we had skipped the concession stands on the concourse and were preparing to hunker down for a three-hour marathon of live wrestling, we didn't hold back. I handed the vendor a wad of bills to cover a massive haul: two sodas, two bottled waters, and two boxes of popcorn per person.

"You got it!" the vendor said, quickly counting the cash, handing me my change, and beginning the monumental task of unloading his tray.

Because we were sitting shoulder-to-shoulder in the dead center of the front row, the logistics of distributing twenty-four individual concession items down the line was a comical team effort. I took the first two boxes of popcorn and handed them left to Jessica. Then two sodas. Then two waters. She wedged the drinks securely under her seat and balanced the popcorn on her lap, effectively trapped but entirely happy about it.

Next came Rachel's haul, then Kristin's, and finally my own.

By the time the vendor moved on, shouting about his ice-cold beer to the next section, we had constructed a literal fortress of snacks. Rachel had her foam finger tucked securely under one arm, her isolation headphones resting around her neck, and a mountain of popcorn and drinks acting as a perfect, physical barrier in the incredibly tight seating arrangement. For an autistic nervous system that thrived on predictable boundaries, the snack fortress was actually an incredibly comforting perimeter.

Inside the ring, the dark matches delivered exactly what they promised.

There were no long, dramatic promos or complicated storylines—just pure, high-octane athleticism. The rookie tag team hit a dizzying sequence of dropkicks and arm drags against a pair of masked local wrestlers, sending the crowd into easy, rhythmic applause. Every time a wrestler hit the mat, the shockwave traveled down through the steel ring posts, vibrating the concrete floor beneath our boots.

It was a masterclass in crowd psychology. The AWE producers knew exactly how to warm up an audience, pacing the action so the cheers grew steadily louder with every near-fall and high-flying maneuver.

Rachel was mesmerized. She sat happily eating her popcorn, her eyes tracking every single footwork adjustment and wrist-lock in the ring. I leaned back in my padded chair, drinking a freezing cold soda and rubbing my tired legs, the physical exhaustion of carrying Jessica down the stairs temporarily masked by the sheer, buzzing energy of the arena.

The referee's hand hit the mat for the final three-count of the dark matches just as the digital clock above the entrance stage flipped to 5:55 PM.

The ring announcer climbed through the ropes, his voice echoing through the massive PA system.

"Ladies and gentlemen... we are five minutes away from going LIVE on national television! Portland, Oregon... ARE YOU READY FOR THE MONDAY NIGHT WAR?!"

The Rose City Center absolutely exploded. The dark matches had done their job perfectly. The crowd was hot, our snack perimeter was fully stocked, and the red light on the massive broadcast cameras began to blink.

## Chapter 3: AWE Monday Night War (Monday, November 20, 2006)

### Part 1: LIVE From Portland – 6:00 PM (PST)

At exactly six o'clock, the ambient hum of the Rose City Center was instantly obliterated.

High above the entrance stage, the massive TitanTron flared to life. The metallic AWE logo slammed onto the screen, sparking with digital interference and a heavy bass drop.
