The sheer auditory force of the bass vibrated straight through the concrete and into the soles of my boots. Beside me, Rachel immediately slid her heavy isolation headphones over her ears, completely locked into her element, her hands gripping her screen-used foam finger. I mirrored her motion, securing my own headphones to filter out the deafening roar of the 14,000 fans as the arena lights went completely dark.

On the massive screen, a high-octane montage of heavy bumps, championship celebrations, and intense brawling played out to a driving, mid-2000s hard rock anthem. The crowd surrounding our front-row barricade went absolutely ballistic, screaming in anticipation as the sequence ended with the iconic MONDAY NIGHT WAR logo shattering the screen.

Normally, this would be the exact microsecond the stage would erupt in a terrifying, concussive volley of fireworks. But tonight, the arena remained dark and mercifully free of the sudden explosions that usually sent our nervous systems into a tailspin.

Instead, the broadcast transitioned to a gritty, slightly desaturated video package. Text appeared in the lower third of the giant screen: LAST WEEK.

The footage showed AWE General Manager Marcus Sterling standing in the center of the ring. He was wearing a sharply tailored, disgustingly expensive suit , looking down his nose at the camera with a smug, patronizing glare. Even through the heavy plastic shells of my headphones, I could hear the Portland crowd instantly erupt into a wall of venomous, localized heat at the sight of the corporate heel.

"Effective immediately, all arena pyrotechnics are banned from AWE programming," Sterling declared on the tape, his voice echoing cleanly through the massive PA system. "The fireworks are over. The 'Resistance' is over. Welcome to the bottom line".

The video package violently cut to black with the echoing sound of a slamming steel vault door.

The pyrotechnic ban was officially in effect, keeping our sensory perimeter perfectly safe, and Monday Night WAR was live on the air.

### Part 2: The Bottom Line – 6:02 PM (PST)

The TitanTron faded to black. For a split second, the massive arena held its breath.

Then, "In Control" hit the arena PA system. It was a slick, aggressively sterile corporate-rock anthem that belonged in a boardroom, not a wrestling ring. The sheer excitement in the Rose City Center instantly curdled into a physical wall of venomous, localized heat.

The crowd absolutely despised Marcus Sterling. We all did. He was the corporate parasite ruining our favorite show.

Sterling stepped through the entrance curtain, pausing at the top of the ramp. He slowly adjusted his French cuffs with a sickening, calculated arrogance, looking out at the 14,000 screaming Oregonians as if they were a stain on his expensive leather shoes.

As he strolled down the long ramp, the hatred only intensified. Next to me, Kristin and Jessica were mercilessly booing him. Rachel, though entirely non-verbal in this chaotic environment, thrust her hands forward, giving the General Manager two emphatic, highly aggressive thumbs down, her giant foam finger waving in protest.

Sterling stepped into the ring and walked dead center. He raised a microphone to his mouth, but the Portland crowd refused to let him speak. The second the metal touched his lips, the tipping point was reached.

It started somewhere in the upper deck—a low, rhythmic rumble that quickly cascaded down through the lower bowl until all 14,000 fans were united in a single, deafening, hostile chant that shook the television cameras. It was the absolute centerpiece of our collective outrage.

"ASS-HOLE! ASS-HOLE! ASS-HOLE! ASS-HOLE!"

Through my heavy isolation headphones, the chant felt like a physical shockwave vibrating against my chest. Next to me, Kristin and Jessica were enthusiastically joining in, screaming the word right along with the rest of the arena. Rachel, fully understanding the assignment, pumped her foam finger to the exact rhythm of the profanity.

Sterling didn't flinch. He just stood dead center in the ring, holding the microphone at his side, soaking in the absolute venom of 14,000 people calling him exactly what he was. He loved every single second of it.

He slowly brought the microphone back to his lips, a cold, condescending smirk cutting across his face.

"Are you quite finished?" Sterling asked, his voice echoing cleanly through the massive PA system, dripping with disdain.

The crowd responded with another tidal wave of boos.

"Portland, Oregon... Listen to yourselves," Sterling continued, pacing the canvas like a king surveying peasants. "You sound like a bunch of spoiled, ungrateful children."

"WHAT?!" the crowd fired back in absolute unison, executing the legendary, weaponized interruption perfectly.

Sterling paused, his left eye twitching just a fraction. He hated that chant. "You're angry..."

"WHAT?!"

"You bought your tickets tonight expecting a flashy, expensive light show..."

"WHAT?!"

"You wanted the explosions..."

"WHAT?!"

"I said you wanted the explosions!" Sterling barked, his calm facade finally cracking as he pointed an angry finger at the front row. "But let me explain the real world to you mouth-breathers! AWE was bleeding money!"

"WHAT?!"

"Stop saying what!" Sterling roared, his face flushing red as the Rose City Center erupted in mocking laughter. He took a deep breath, smoothing the lapels of his suit to regain his composure. He glared directly at the hard camera, completely unfazed by the vitriol raining down on him.

"I was brought in to get out-of-control costs under control!" Sterling yelled, projecting iron-clad authority. "And my first official act was to ban the pyrotechnics because you don't deserve them! I am trimming the fat! I am protecting the bottom line!"

The boos grew louder, shaking the steel barricade just inches from our knees.

"I don't care if you boo me out of this building!" Sterling sneered over the jeers. "I am building a corporate machine that doesn't need fireworks to succeed! Because I hold the contracts. I hold the power. And quite frankly, I am the only reason any of you people have a show to watch tonight!"

### Part 3: The Hometown Hero – 6:08 PM (PST)

Marcus Sterling stood in the center of the ring, basking in the absolute hatred of the Portland crowd. He had all the power, and he wanted every single person in the arena to know it.

Suddenly, a heavy, distorted guitar riff shredded through the arena PA system, drowning out Sterling’s arrogant sneer.

The Rose City Center didn't just cheer. The building completely came unglued. The pop was so incredibly massive, so viscerally loud, that I actually felt the steel barricade rattle against my knees.

Stepping out onto the entrance stage was Jaxson "The Outlaw" Vance.

He didn't wear a shiny corporate suit or a flashy, sequined robe. He was wearing taped wrists, worn denim jeans, and a faded leather vest. He was the gritty, blue-collar soul of the AWE, and more importantly for tonight, he was born and raised just ten minutes down Highway 26 in Beaverton. This was his house.

Next to me, Rachel was practically levitating out of her seat, aggressively waving her foam finger as her hometown hero marched down the ramp.

Vance didn't play to the crowd or hype them up. He looked dead-eyed and serious. He climbed the steel steps, stepped through the ropes, and walked right up to the General Manager. Sterling cautiously took a half-step back as Vance raised a microphone to his lips, his chest heaving, his voice raw with authentic emotion.

"You know, Marcus..." Vance growled, his voice cutting through the cheers. "I was sitting in the back, listening to you call my friends and my family out here in Portland 'spoiled children.' You sit up in your corporate suite and you fire good men just so you can save a few bucks to buy another ugly suit."

The crowd roared in agreement. Vance paced a tight circle around the executive.

"You banned the pyro because you're cheap," Vance continued, stopping dead in his tracks and staring Sterling down. "And you hide behind Silas Thorne's World Championship to protect your precious bottom line."

Sterling’s eye twitched again. He quickly motioned for a ringside technician to hand him another microphone. He snatched it through the ropes, his face flushing with anger.

"My team is anchored by 'The Blueprint' Silas Thorne!" Sterling sneered, leaning in close but keeping a safe distance from Vance's fists. "He is genetically superior to you in every conceivable way, Vance. You're just a glorified bar brawler from a suburb that doesn't matter."

Vance smiled—a dangerous, knowing smile that instantly signaled to the 14,000 fans that he had Sterling exactly where he wanted him.

"A glorified bar brawler?" Vance asked quietly, tilting his head. "If you really think your golden boy is unstoppable... prove it. Put Silas Thorne in this ring with me. Tonight. For the AWE World Heavyweight Championship."

The pop was deafening. The idea of getting a World Title match on free television instead of a Sunday Pay-Per-View was the holy grail for a live crowd. Jessica leaned forward in her chair, her jaw dropping, while Kristin grabbed my arm and shook it in sheer excitement.

Sterling let out a high-pitched, nervous laugh, shaking his head. "You're delusional, Vance. Risk my World Champion in a title match six days before a Pay-Per-View? I make the decisions, not you!"

"You're terrified, Marcus," Vance shot back, cutting off his escape route. "You know if I beat your boy, your whole corporate facade crumbles. So I'll make you a deal. Put the World Title on the line tonight. If Silas beats me, my team will forfeit at No Quarter, and you can keep your cheap, pathetic pyro ban forever."

Vance took a heavy step forward, pointing a taped finger firmly into the center of Sterling's chest.

"But... when I beat Silas Thorne tonight... I walk out of Portland as the new AWE World Champion, AND the stakes for No Quarter change. If your team loses this Sunday, the pyrotechnic ban is lifted forever."

It was a strange, paradoxical moment for our section of the front row. We absolutely needed that pyro ban to stay in effect for Rachel’s neurological safety. But trapped in the infectious, chaotic energy of the arena, surrounded by the deafening roar of our hometown crowd, we were completely swept up in the narrative.

The chant began immediately, rolling down from the upper deck and hitting the ring like a battering ram.

"COWARD! COWARD! COWARD! COWARD!"

Kristin, Jessica, and I joined right in, screaming at the top of our lungs. Rachel pumped her giant foam finger to the exact rhythm of the chant, completely lost in the magic of the show.

Sterling's corporate composure completely shattered under the weight of his own massive ego and the thunderous, undeniable taunting of 14,000 fans. His face turned a deep, furious crimson. He raised the microphone, completely unhinged.

"I AM NOT A COWARD!" Sterling screamed, his voice cracking violently through the PA system. "You think you can blackmail me?! You think you can beat The Blueprint?! You want the title?! You want your little sparklers on the line for Sunday?! FINE! You got it!"

The arena floor literally shook as the crowd realized what had just happened.

"Tonight's main event!" Sterling roared over the pandemonium. "Silas Thorne versus Jaxson Vance for the AWE World Championship! And when Silas breaks you in half, the pyro ban is permanent!"

The Rose City Center absolutely exploded. The volume was astronomical, a pure, unadulterated wall of sound.

Jaxson Vance didn't celebrate. He just offered Sterling one last, gritty smirk, dropped his microphone onto the canvas with a heavy thud, and simply walked away.

We had a World Heavyweight Championship main event.

### Part 4: The Cruiserweight Crash – 6:18 PM (PST)

It took a few minutes for the arena to calm down after Jaxson Vance and Marcus Sterling cleared the ring. The lingering buzz of the hometown main event announcement was still thick in the air when the lights swept over the crowd again.

A fast-paced, electronic Lucha Libre track hit the PA system. El Relámpago sprinted out from behind the curtain, wearing a brilliant silver and blue mask that caught the arena spotlights. He didn't even use the steel stairs; he slid seamlessly under the bottom rope and scaled the turnbuckle in one fluid motion, playing to the crowd.

Seconds later, an upbeat punk rock anthem blasted through the speakers, and Johnny "Air" Strike burst onto the stage. He was absolutely buzzing with adrenaline, sprinting down the ramp and slapping the outstretched hands of the fans leaning over the barricade.

The bell rang, and the match instantly shifted into fifth gear.

Without the television commentary of Dallas Ward and Cal Stone filling our ears to explain the stakes, watching a cruiserweight match from the front row was an entirely different, incredibly raw experience. It wasn't a television show anymore; it was a high-speed athletic collision happening right in our laps.

We could hear everything. We heard the sharp squeak of their boots against the canvas. We heard the violent, echoing SMACK of knife-edge chops echoing through the building. Neither man stayed on the mat for more than two seconds, trading arm drags and lightning-fast reversals in a blinding flurry of chain wrestling.

About ten minutes into the bout, the fight spilled to the outside.

Johnny Strike took a vicious, spinning enzuigiri kick directly to the side of the head. He tumbled awkwardly through the middle ropes, his boots hitting the thin black floor mats with a heavy thud. He stumbled backward, completely dazed, until his spine slammed right into the steel barricade dead center of our section.

He was standing exactly a foot in front of us.

Because we were wearing our heavy isolation headphones around our necks to filter out the crowd noise when needed, the auditory experience of the athletes was incredibly intimate. I could hear Johnny violently gasping for air, his chest heaving. I could see the sweat dripping off his chin and the red, raw welts forming across his chest.

Inside the ring, El Relámpago saw his opening. He hit the far ropes, building up terrifying, uncontrolled speed.

I saw exactly what was about to happen.

Relámpago launched his body like a silver-and-blue missile, diving straight through the middle and top ropes.

It was a catastrophic collision. Relámpago's skull and shoulder crashed directly into Johnny's chest. The sheer, violent momentum of a two-hundred-pound man flying through the air sent Johnny violently backward.

The heavy steel barricade bowed inward toward us with a deafening, metallic CRASH.

I knew arena rules—you never reach over or touch the barricade—but I wasn't going to let that steel crush my sisters. Instantly, I shifted my weight forward. Planting my heavy work boots into the concrete, I threw my arms protectively across Jessica and Rachel like a heavy human seatbelt. I braced my entire body just inches from the bowing metal, ready to take the full blunt-force impact myself if the iron hinges snapped.

The barricade violently shuddered and stopped, holding steady a mere two inches from my knees.

Jessica didn't even flinch. She just sat back in her padded folding chair and watched the action, completely trusting my physical block. Beside me, Rachel grabbed the sleeve of my shirt to ground herself, her eyes wide as the two wrestlers collapsed in a heap of tangled limbs on the thin ringside mats right at our feet.

To the millions watching on TV, it looked like a perfectly executed, flawless stunt. But down here on the floor, the reality of the impact was brutal.

I could clearly hear the unamplified, desperate whuff of air leaving Johnny's lungs as the wind was completely knocked out of him. The referee slid out of the ring, crouching right in front of us.

"You good? You good?" the referee whispered urgently, his hand resting lightly on Johnny's back.

Johnny offered a microscopic nod. Relámpago, untangling his legs from the barricade, leaned in close to Johnny's ear and rapidly whispered the next sequence in breathless Spanish to set up the finish.

Relámpago scrambled to his feet, grabbed Johnny by the hair, and rolled him back under the bottom rope. He scaled the turnbuckle and launched himself into a breathtaking, picture-perfect 450 Splash.

The referee’s hand hit the mat three times.

The timekeeper rang the bell, and El Relámpago’s fast-paced Lucha theme hit the PA system again. The Portland crowd erupted into a massive, appreciative roar for the incredible opening bout.

As the noise level in the arena spiked violently, Rachel and I quickly slid our heavy isolation headphones fully over our ears. The thundering applause faded back into a manageable, muffled hum.

I pulled my arm back from my sisters, rolling my shoulders and exhaling a sharp breath. The show was only twenty minutes in, and the war zone had already reached our front door.

### Part 5: The Corporate Meltdown – 6:30 PM (PST)

After a quick commercial break for the television audience, the arena lights dimmed once again. The massive TitanTron above the entrance stage flared to life, and the broadcast seamlessly transitioned to a backstage camera feed.

The Portland crowd immediately erupted into a chorus of heavy boos.

The camera opened on a tight, high-definition shot of a massive, professionally lit oil painting of General Manager Marcus Sterling. In the ridiculous portrait, Sterling was standing on a mountain peak, wearing a pristine tuxedo, looking thoughtfully into the distance like a conquering emperor.

The camera slowly panned out to reveal the rest of his luxury backstage office. It was a masterclass in excessive, obnoxious corporate wealth. There was a polished mahogany desk, imported white leather couches, and no fewer than three more framed, high-gloss portraits of Marcus Sterling adorning the walls.

Sterling was standing behind his desk, pouring himself a glass of sparkling water. He looked slightly flushed from his confrontation in the ring, but he was trying desperately to project an aura of total, unbothered calm.

BANG.

The audio of the heavy oak door being violently kicked open echoed through the arena PA system.

"The Blueprint" Silas Thorne stormed into the frame, and the crowd noise shifted from boos to an excited, mocking roar. The AWE World Heavyweight Champion was usually the picture of arrogant perfection, but right now, he looked absolutely unhinged. He was wearing his immaculate, tailored three-piece suit, but his tie was pulled loose, his hair was uncharacteristically messy, and he was clutching the massive gold World Championship belt to his chest like a life preserver in a hurricane.

"Are you out of your mind?!" Silas screamed, the veins bulging visibly in his neck on the fifty-foot screen. "Are you completely, medically insane?!"

Sterling winced, holding up a manicured hand. "Silas. Lower your voice. You're going to wrinkle the Italian wool."

"I don't care about the suit! I care about this!" Silas roared, slamming his free hand onto the mahogany desk. He shoved the World Championship belt directly into Sterling's face. "I am the AWE World Heavyweight Champion! I am the face of your franchise! And you just put my title on the line against a deranged, taped-up brawler just because he hurt your feelings in front of a bunch of lumberjacks!"

Next to me, Kristin let out a loud, genuine laugh. Silas was completely losing his grip, and it was incredible television.

On the screen, Sterling smoothly pushed the heavy gold belt away and took a calm sip of his water. He leaned back against his desk, projecting total corporate arrogance.

"Silas, please. Sit down," Sterling said, his voice dripping with condescension. "You are overreacting. I didn't gamble anything. I made a strategic corporate maneuver. Vance thinks he trapped me, but all he did was walk right into a buzzsaw."

"A buzzsaw?!" Silas yelled, frantically pacing the length of the lavish office. "Have you listened to that crowd out there?! Vance is a madman, Marcus! And we're twenty minutes from his hometown! If he hits me with that lariat, he takes my title, he lifts your pyrotechnic ban, and our entire team looks weak going into No Quarter!"

Sterling just chuckled. It was a cold, infuriating sound.

"Do you really think I'd leave our most valuable asset unprotected?" Sterling asked, walking over and placing a reassuring hand on the champion's expensive shoulder. "Do you really think I'm going to let some blue-collar hoodlum walk out of Portland with my World Championship?"

The entire arena leaned in.

"You aren't going out there alone tonight, Silas," Sterling promised, a wicked smile spreading across his face. "I've already spoken to Avalanche and Carter Finnley. They are going to be stationed at ringside for the entire main event. If Vance even breathes in a way I don't like, Avalanche is going to tear his head off while the referee is distracted."

The Rose City Center instantly erupted into a deafening wall of heat. It was blatant, cowardly cheating, perfectly designed to make us hate the corporate faction even more.

Sterling patted Silas on the shoulder and walked back to his desk, carefully adjusting one of the framed photos of himself.

"So go get your gear on. Stretch. Relax," Sterling ordered smoothly. "Tonight is going to be a piece of cake. We embarrass Vance in his own backyard, we keep the title, we secure the pyro ban, and we ride that momentum straight through Sunday."

Sterling sat down in his plush leather chair, completely satisfied with his own brilliance.

But the backstage camera didn't stay on the General Manager. It slowly zoomed in, lingering tightly on Silas Thorne's face.

Despite Sterling's iron-clad promises of outside interference, the champion did not look reassured. The fifty-foot high-definition screen broadcasted his absolute dread to the entire arena. Silas stared down at the gold championship belt in his hands. He swallowed hard, his eyes darting back and forth as his mind calculated the risk.

The sheer panic was painted vividly across his features. The Blueprint knew exactly what 14,000 fans in Portland already knew: Marcus Sterling had completely lost control of the situation, and Jaxson Vance was coming to collect.

### Part 6: The Riot vs. The Empress – 6:40 PM (PST)

After the backstage feed of Marcus Sterling's meltdown faded from the TitanTron, the arena lights shifted, bathing the 14,000 fans in a deep, regal purple hue.

A grandiose, highly pretentious remix of classical harpsichord music echoed through the PA system, accompanied by a heavy, driving bassline.

"The Empress" Victoria Hale stepped through the entrance curtain. She didn't walk down the ramp; she glided. She was draped in a lavish, faux-ermine robe, and perched perfectly atop her head was a glittering, gold-plated crown. She was closely trailed by a terrified-looking male attendant carrying a velvet pillow, upon which rested a heavy, jewel-encrusted scepter.

She stopped at the bottom of the ramp, looking out at the Portland crowd with an expression of pure, concentrated disgust, as if the oxygen in the arena was entirely beneath her standards.

Suddenly, the pretentious classical music was violently cut off by a blaring air raid siren, followed instantly by a frantic, heavily distorted punk rock track.

The crowd absolutely erupted. Next to me, Kristin shot out of her folding chair, enthusiastically pointing to the new, studded-graphic Roxy Dane t-shirt she had just picked out at the merchandise stand.

Roxy "The Riot" Dane exploded through the curtain like a cannonball. She was a ball of pure kinetic energy, wearing taped fists, kickboxing trunks, and a studded leather vest. She sprinted down the ramp, high-fiving the fans along the barricade, practically vibrating with adrenaline. She slid smoothly under the bottom rope, bounced off the far turnbuckle, and began aggressively shadowboxing in the center of the canvas.

Victoria Hale looked at her like she was a rodent.

The referee called for the bell, and Roxy didn't waste a single microsecond.

Because the ambient noise of the crowd was so overwhelming, Rachel and I had our heavy isolation headphones firmly locked over our ears. But sitting dead center in the front row, our ears were perfectly level with the ring apron. The headphones filtered out the 14,000 screaming fans, but they couldn't block out the immediate, acoustic violence of the ring itself.

When Roxy closed the distance and launched a blistering inside leg kick, it didn't look or sound like a choreographed television performance. It sounded like a baseball bat hitting a side of beef.

THWACK.

The heavy, dull echo of Roxy's shin bone colliding with Hale's thigh reverberated sharply off the taut canvas. It was the brutal reality of Japanese strong-style striking—they were genuinely hitting each other, just protecting the vital targets.

Jessica leaned forward in her wheelchair, her eyes wide, completely captivated by the sheer physical intensity unfolding five feet in front of us.

Roxy systematically dismantled the Empress's base, targeting the lead leg with a wince-inducing combination of stiff jabs and heavy kicks to prevent Hale from shooting for a technical takedown.

Desperate to stop the flurry, Hale retreated toward the ropes. In a brilliant piece of heel psychology, she used the referee's body to block his line of sight, quickly and viciously raking Roxy's eyes. Roxy stumbled backward, temporarily blinded. Hale instantly capitalized, sweeping Roxy's damaged leg and locking in a brutal, grounded surfboard stretch, pulling back on Roxy's arms with incredible leverage.

Even through the headphones, I could hear Roxy grunting in pain. The Portland crowd rallied behind her, starting a heavy, rhythmic clapping that shook the floorboards.

Roxy fed off the energy. Using her incredible core strength, she rolled violently forward, breaking Hale's grip and scrambling back to her feet.

Infuriated that her technical submission had been broken, Victoria Hale rolled under the bottom rope directly in front of us. She snatched the heavy, jewel-encrusted scepter right off her attendant's velvet pillow.

"She's got the scepter!" Kristin yelled over the crowd noise, though the referee's back was completely turned as he checked on Roxy.

Hale slid back into the ring, stalking up behind Roxy with the heavy metal prop raised high. She swung it like a baseball bat directly at the back of Roxy's head.

But Roxy's ring awareness was flawless. At the absolute last microsecond, she dropped her center of gravity, ducking cleanly under the swinging scepter. The momentum spun the Empress completely around, leaving her wide open.

Roxy unleashed her signature flurry. Right hook. Left hook. A spinning back elbow to the jaw that echoed like a gunshot.

As Hale dropped to her knees, completely dazed and dropping her scepter, Roxy bounced off the ropes, built up maximum speed, and delivered a devastating, running knee strike flush to the side of the Empress's head. Hale collapsed onto the mat, completely motionless.

Roxy hooked the leg. The referee dropped to the canvas.

ONE! TWO! THREE!

The timekeeper slammed the hammer into the bell, and Roxy's punk rock anthem blasted through the arena. The crowd exploded. Kristin and Jessica threw their arms in the air, cheering wildly, while Rachel clapped her hands in a rapid, joyous rhythm, her giant foam finger waving proudly in the air.

On the massive TitanTron, the graphic flashed: ROXY DANE WINS! ADVANCES TO NO QUARTER.

Roxy leaped onto the nearest turnbuckle, throwing her taped fists into the air to celebrate with the Portland crowd. She was going to the Pay-Per-View to fight for the Women's Championship, and our front-row seats had just given us an unforgettable, front-row masterclass in controlled chaos.

### Part 7: The Tycoon and the Thief – 6:55 PM (PST)

As the ringside crew rushed out to sweep the canvas and check the ropes after Roxy Dane’s victory, the arena lights dimmed once again.

The massive TitanTron above the entrance stage flickered, displaying a graphic that read: LIVE: MAIN CONCOURSE.

"Look!" Kristin shouted, grabbing Jessica's shoulder and pointing up at the fifty-foot screen. "It's us!"

Sitting dead center in the front row, we tilted our heads back and looked up at the massive monitor. It was a surreal, out-of-body experience. Thanks to the magic of television production, the footage we had filmed nearly three hours earlier in a completely empty hallway was now being broadcast to the world as if it were happening at this exact microsecond.

On the massive screen, the heavy double doors down the concourse banged open. "Sly" Diego Chavez burst into the frame, jogging backward and flashing his signature, million-dollar smirk. He was waving a platinum money clip stuffed with thick, green hundred-dollar bills.

"Chavez!" a voice roared through the PA system.

Stomping into the light was "The Tycoon" Preston Sinclair. The billionaire heel was dressed in his pristine white Texan suit, his face contorted in absolute fury. But instead of a microphone, Sinclair was dragging a massive, thick-braided Texas Bull Rope behind him, a heavy iron cowbell clanking violently against the concrete floor.

"You think you can steal from me?!" Sinclair bellowed, winding the thick rope around his wrist. "I'm going to skin you alive this Sunday!"

"You gotta catch me first, Tycoon!" Diego yelled back, seamlessly hyping their upcoming Pay-Per-View bout.

The Portland crowd popped massively as Sinclair lunged forward, swinging the heavy bull rope with terrifying speed. The cowbell whipped through the air, but Diego nimbly dropped to a knee, ducking the strike perfectly. The rope smashed loudly against a concrete pillar instead, echoing through the concourse.

Diego just laughed, hopping effortlessly onto the top of the merchandise table. Sinclair tried to charge, but two AWE security extras rushed into the frame, holding the furious billionaire back.

"Oye, Portland!" Diego shouted, looking down directly into the camera lens. "Let me tell you a little secret about my good friend, Preston Sinclair! He thinks tying me up in a Texas Bull Rope Match this Sunday at No Quarter is gonna keep me down! But the man is incredibly generous! In fact, Señor Sinclair just told me he wants to buy every single person in this line some brand new gear! On him!"

The camera cut to the reverse angle. There we were, fifty feet tall on the TitanTron.

We hit our cue perfectly. Kristin, Jessica, and I were cheering loudly, but the camera lingered on Rachel. She was completely lost in the magic of the moment, jumping up and down, clapping her hands in a flurry of uncontainable excitement. Sitting next to me in the arena, Rachel gasped, clutching her actual foam finger tightly as she watched herself on national television.

"Give the kid in the front a hat!" Diego shouted on the screen, tossing the classic AWE cap directly into my hands.

"Give the lady in the back a foam finger, homes!" Diego yelled. He tossed the giant, blue-and-silver foam prop right to Rachel. On the screen, she caught it, clutching it to her chest like it was made of solid gold.

Diego turned to the bewildered merchandise cashier, peeling a thick stack of bills off Sinclair's platinum clip and slapping them onto the table. "Keep the change, amigo! The Tycoon is paying for it!"

Suddenly, the massive screen above the stage split perfectly in half.

On the right side of the TitanTron, the pre-taped footage showed Diego happily tossing out free merchandise to our ecstatic group. But on the left side, a live camera fed in from the backstage area.

Preston Sinclair was standing in front of a backstage monitor, watching the broadcast in real-time. His face was turning an apocalyptic, veins-popping shade of purple.

"Those are my hundreds!" Sinclair screamed in pure, unadulterated agony, his voice cracking violently over the PA system. "That is my hard-earned capital! You filthy, lying, cheating thief! I'm going to destroy you on Sunday! I'M CALLING MY LAWYERS!"

The arena erupted into mocking, deafening laughter as Sinclair ripped his pristine white suit jacket off in a furious, childlike tantrum and stomped on it right there on the backstage floor.

### Part 8: The Top of the Hour – 7:00 PM (PST)

Exactly one hour into the live broadcast, the commercial break ended. The arena lights swept across the roaring Portland crowd, and the massive TitanTron flashed a brand new graphic: *NO QUARTER – 5-ON-5 ELIMINATION MATCH PREVIEW*.

A crisp, obnoxiously polished brass-and-percussion track hit the PA system. The crowd instantly responded with a chorus of heavy, irritated boos.

Stepping out onto the stage were Avery and Hayes—The Board of Directors. Representing Marcus Sterling’s corporate faction, they wore custom-tailored warm-up suits that easily cost a small fortune. They strutted down the long ramp, looking at the blue-collar Portland fans with utter, undisguised disdain.

But as they reached the ring, the arena spotlights swept back up to the top of the entrance stage, revealing a massive, unexpected production change.

A four-piece band, bathed in cyan and violet neon lights, was fully set up next to the TitanTron. There was a massive drum kit, a heavy bass guitar, and a sprawling bank of vintage synthesizers. The broadcast desk announced them as Willamette Valley's own synth-rock sensation, The Velvet Static.

The drummer clicked his sticks together, counting off the tempo, and the band launched into a blistering, heavy 1980s Darkwave track.

Down in the front row, the sheer, concussive physical power of a live band inside an indoor arena was immense. Rachel and I immediately clamped our heavy isolation headphones firmly over our ears, the thick foam shells completely filtering out the sharp, painful auditory spikes of the live drum kit.

But we couldn't filter out the bass.

The deep, synthesized Darkwave rhythm was so heavy it felt like a physical entity. I could feel the low-end frequency rattling my ribs and vibrating aggressively up through the thick rubber soles of my boots. I looked over at Rachel, but she was completely in her element. She offered a massive smile, tapping her fingers happily on her knees in perfect, flawless time with the live rhythm.

Elias and Rex—The Midnight Runners—burst onto the stage right in front of the band.

When the graphic flashed on the screen billing them from *GRESHAM, OREGON*, the Rose City Center absolutely erupted. The hometown boys, who had probably grown up riding the same Columbia Transit System buses we used to navigate around the city today, were wearing identical black leather trench coats, identical boots, and matching long hair. They sprinted down the ramp, full of kinetic energy, and slid into the ring.

The bell rang, and the local momentum was entirely on the side of the Midnight Runners. Elias started the match against Avery, hitting a beautiful, fluid arm-drag, following it up with a dropkick, and tagging in Rex for a stunning double-team suplex.

But the Board of Directors quickly resorted to their corporate playbook.

As Rex hit the ropes right in front of us, Hayes blatantly grabbed his ankle from the outside. Rex stumbled hard. Avery capitalized instantly, delivering a vicious chop block to the back of Rex's knee that echoed through the building.

For the next five minutes, Avery and Hayes put on an absolute clinic in classic, methodical tag-team psychology. They dragged Rex into their corner, cutting the ring perfectly in half and isolating him completely. While the referee was distracted warning Avery, Hayes aggressively choked Rex on the bottom rope.

Rex was getting battered. Avery picked him up and threw him hard out of the ring, specifically tossing him to the floor on the far side of the ring, away from the hard television camera.

Hayes jumped onto the ring apron and started screaming directly in the referee's face, completely drawing the official's attention away from the floor.

Sitting in the front row, we had a perfect angle of what happened next.

Down on the floor mats, Rex grabbed the edge of the black ring apron and quickly scrambled completely under the ring to "hide" from Avery. A split-second later, a wrestler in identical gear and identical long hair slid out from under the opposite side of the apron, clutching his ribs and acting incredibly groggy.

It was Elias. The classic "Twin Magic" switch.

Kristin and Jessica burst out laughing. The live crowd absolutely loved it, cheering wildly for the blatant cheating because it was their hometown heroes outsmarting the corporate suits.

Avery, completely unaware of the switch, rolled under the bottom rope, grabbed the "groggy" brother by the hair, and tossed him back into the ring.

Avery went for a lazy, arrogant clothesline to finish the match. But the perfectly fresh, fully rested Elias easily ducked under it. He hit the ropes and exploded with a massive, high-speed flying forearm! Hayes ran in to interfere, but Elias caught him with a devastating back-body drop that sent him flying halfway across the ring.

Elias dragged a stunned Avery to the corner and tagged the real Rex—who had just casually crawled back out from under the ring and hopped onto the apron.

The brothers launched into the air simultaneously, catching Avery in their devastating tandem finisher, the Total Eclipse.

Rex hooked the leg. *ONE! TWO! THREE!*

The live band on the stage instantly fired up the victory chord, and the hometown crowd unleashed a massive, triumphant roar.

Inside the ring, Elias and Rex had their arms raised in victory. But on the outside, the scene turned ugly very quickly. Avery and Hayes were humiliated, furious, and clutching their jaws as they stumbled up the entrance ramp. Their tailored warm-up suits were ruined, and the localized Portland fans surrounding the stage began mercilessly mocking them.

Hayes completely snapped.

He marched right up to the lead singer of The Velvet Static, who was happily strumming his guitar, and viciously shoved the musician flat onto his back.

The crowd gasped.

Avery grabbed the heavy bass guitar by the neck, hoisted it over his head, and violently smashed it against the steel stage grating. The wood shattered into a dozen jagged pieces with a sickening crunch.

Not to be outdone, Hayes delivered a brutal kick to the vintage synthesizer, knocking it entirely off its stand. The heavy keyboard crashed to the concrete floor in a shower of sparks and broken plastic, cutting the live music dead in an instant.

The cheers instantly transformed into nuclear, white-hot heat.

Avery and Hayes stood over the broken instruments and the stunned band members, glaring fiercely down the long ramp at the Midnight Runners. Referees were already flooding the aisle, desperately holding Elias and Rex back from charging up the ramp.

Team Sterling was sending a massive, highly destructive message ahead of *No Quarter*. And as the boos rained down on the broken stage, I couldn't help but think that the quiet, peaceful ARTS train ride back to Washington tomorrow was going to feel like a completely different universe compared to this war zone.

### Part 9: The Backstage Assault – 7:15 PM (PST)

As the ringside crew scrambled down the ramp to sweep up the splintered remains of the bass guitar and drag the broken synthesizer away, the massive TitanTron above the stage flickered.

The live feed cut backstage, but the pristine, high-gloss lighting of Marcus Sterling’s luxury office was entirely gone.

Instead, we were looking at the Resistance locker room. It was dimly lit, concrete-walled, and purely functional. The Portland crowd roared at the sight of their hometown hero, Jaxson "The Outlaw" Vance. He was sitting on a metal folding chair, methodically wrapping his wrists in white athletic tape. He looked intensely focused, staring a hole right through the concrete floor.

Pacing back and forth in front of him like a caged tiger was the grizzled veteran, "Iron" Tommy Brooks. Even on the fifty-foot screen, you could clearly see the heavily scarred tissue around his forehead and eyes—the unmistakable hallmark of a man who had spent twenty years bleeding for the industry.

Deep in the background, sitting cross-legged in the shadows on top of a row of metal lockers, was Fracture. He was completely silent, wearing his tattered mask, slowly and rhythmically whittling a piece of scrap wood with a pocketknife.

"Sterling thinks he's playing chess," Brooks growled, his gravelly, intense voice echoing cleanly through the arena PA system. "He thinks he can just wave his hand and send Avalanche and Carter Finnley down to the ring to steal your World Championship tonight. He thinks he's got the numbers."

Brooks stopped pacing. He knelt down, looking Vance dead in the eyes.

"You listen to me, kid," Brooks said firmly. "You focus on Silas Thorne. You focus on taking that gold and ending this pyro ban for good. If Avalanche or Finnley even *think* about taking a step down that ramp tonight... the Resistance will intercept them. We will tear them apart before they ever reach the barricade. You have my word."

Vance slowly looked up. He ripped the end of the athletic tape with his teeth, spat it out, and gave Brooks a single, determined nod.

Suddenly, a loud crash and off-camera screaming blared through the speakers.

The locker room door on the screen flew open, and a panicked production assistant stuck his head in. "We need help! Out on the loading dock! Avalanche is out of control!"

The TitanTron feed instantly switched to a frantic, handheld tracking shot. The camera operator sprinted down the concrete hallway, following the assistant out to the arena's massive, cavernous loading dock.

It was absolute chaos.

Danny Gibson, a highly popular mid-card babyface, was writhing on the cold concrete floor in pure, unadulterated agony, clutching his right shoulder. Standing directly over him was the 6-foot-9, 320-pound monster known as Avalanche, breathing heavily and looking completely devoid of emotion.

Standing safely behind the giant was the cowardly opportunist, Carter "The Fox" Finnley, laughing and obnoxiously adjusting his designer sunglasses.

Referees and EMTs swarmed the scene, desperately pushing Avalanche back to create space. Gibson was screaming at the top of his lungs that his collarbone was snapped.

Knowing the behind-the-scenes mechanics of the industry, I knew exactly what I was watching. Danny Gibson was suffering from a legitimate, severely torn rotator cuff. This brutal, unannounced backstage assault was the perfect narrative sleight-of-hand to write him off AWE television so he could go undergo real-world surgery. But to the thousands of fans in the Rose City Center, it was a terrifying, unprovoked mauling.

Suddenly, "The Anvil" Craig Bronson kicked open the heavy fire doors. He took one look at his tag-team partner and best friend writhing on the concrete, and he saw red.

Bronson charged straight at Avalanche. Four referees immediately intercepted him, throwing their bodies in front of him and violently shoving him backward to prevent a full-blown brawl right there on the loading dock.

"You want to take cheap shots in the back?!" Bronson screamed over the chaos, pointing furiously at the giant. "You want to break my partner's arm, you cowardly son of a bitch?!"

Carter Finnley stepped out from behind Avalanche, a sickening smirk plastered on his face.

"Take it easy, Anvil," Finnley said smoothly, his voice dripping with condescension. "It's just business. Unfortunately, it looks like your boy Danny can't compete tonight. Which means Avalanche wins via forfeit. See you on Sunday!"

Bronson violently shoved one of the referees aside, stepping right up into Avalanche's massive chest. Avalanche didn't even flinch. He just stared coldly downward at the smaller man.

"There's no forfeit tonight," Bronson snarled, his teeth bared. "If Danny can't go... I'm taking his spot. You and me. Right now. Get out to the ring!"

Bronson turned and stormed down the hallway, heading straight for the entrance curtain. Finnley's smirk instantly vanished. He looked up at Avalanche, clearly nervous about the audible, but the giant simply cracked his massive knuckles, completely unbothered by the challenge.

The TitanTron faded to black.

Without the commentary desk explaining the transition, the raw energy of the arena took over. The crowd was buzzing, furious at the ambush but fully hyped for the impromptu grudge match. We were moments away from watching a 320-pound giant step into the ring, and the World Heavyweight Championship main event was looming right behind it.

### Part 10: The Upset of the Century – 7:25 PM (PST)

When the live feed from the loading dock cut out, the arena lights shifted instantly to an ominous, blood-red crimson.

Inside the ring, "The Anvil" Craig Bronson was already pacing like a rabid dog. He was furious, staring a hole through the entrance curtain, waiting to avenge his hospitalized tag team partner.

A heavy, slow drumbeat layered with distorted, grinding guitar riffs hit the PA system. Avalanche slowly walked through the curtain. He wasn't just a wrestler; he was a terrifying, 6-foot-9, 320-pound wall of muscle and malice. Behind him, Carter "The Fox" Finnley strutted down the ramp, grinning and casually adjusting his expensive sunglasses, fully expecting a massacre.

He got one.

The bell rang, and Bronson charged immediately. Operating purely on adrenaline, he threw a flurry of stiff, right-handed haymakers directly at Avalanche's jaw.

Avalanche didn't even bother to block them. He simply absorbed the punches, completely unfazed. He reached out with one massive hand, grabbed Bronson by the throat, and violently hurled the 240-pound brawler halfway across the ring into the turnbuckles like he was a child.

For the next two minutes, it was an absolute slaughter. Without the broadcast desk to contextualize the action, the visual storytelling was brutally clear. Avalanche methodically dismantled The Anvil. He hit a crushing corner splash, followed it up with a brutal military press drop, and slowly stalked around the ring.

Down at ringside, Finnley was laughing hysterically, leaning over the barricade just a few feet from us and yelling at the front row that this was exactly what happened when you messed with Team Sterling.

Inside the ring, Avalanche grabbed Bronson by the throat again. He lifted The Anvil high into the air and drove him straight down into the canvas with a catastrophic Chokeslam.

The impact was devastating.

Down in the front row, Rachel and I physically jolted in our padded folding chairs. With our heavy isolation headphones firmly clamped over our ears, we didn't hear the deafening crack of the ring canvas. Instead, we *felt* it. The massive kinetic shockwave traveled directly down through the steel ring posts, into the concrete floor, and shot straight up through the thick rubber soles of our boots.

Jessica's wheelchair rattled violently against the floor mats. Rachel gripped my forearm tightly, her eyes wide at the sheer, undeniable physical power of the giant.

Avalanche stood over Bronson's broken body. He casually placed a single, heavy boot squarely on Bronson's chest.

The referee dropped to the canvas to count. *ONE! TWO!*

Avalanche casually pulled his foot away, intentionally breaking his own pinfall. The crowd showered him with boos. He wasn't finished liquidating the asset; he wanted to punish Bronson even more.

Avalanche dragged a completely limp Bronson to his feet. He scooped him up onto his massive shoulders, preparing for a devastating running powerslam to finish the job for good.

But as Avalanche took his first heavy step forward, Bronson suddenly came alive.

Operating on absolute, sheer desperation, Bronson violently elbowed Avalanche in the side of the head. He slid down the giant's back and shoved Avalanche forcefully chest-first into the turnbuckle pad.

Avalanche staggered backward, dazed for just a fraction of a second.

Bronson didn't hesitate. He leaped into the air, wrapping his arms and legs completely around Avalanche's massive torso, dropping all of his dead weight backward into a desperate, textbook Crucifix Pin.

Avalanche's shoulders hit the mat. The referee dove into position.

ONE! TWO! THREE!

The Rose City Center didn't just cheer. The building completely came unglued. It was a deafening, sustained roar of pure shock and absolute ecstasy. Rachel and I quickly clamped our hands tightly over the plastic shells of our headphones, physically securing them against the insane, sudden spike in localized volume.

Bronson instantly broke the hold. He rolled backward, scrambled frantically under the bottom rope, and sprinted backward up the entrance ramp, clutching his ribs, his eyes wide in total disbelief.

Inside the ring, Avalanche sat up. He looked at his empty hands. The realization hit him. He snapped his head toward the referee, his expression shifting from momentary confusion to absolute, terrifying rage.

Avalanche charged the official, backing the terrified referee into the corner, screaming in a deep, guttural roar and aggressively tapping his own right shoulder, insisting he had kicked out in time. At ringside, Carter Finnley's jaw had literally dropped to the floor. He jumped onto the ring apron, waving his arms furiously at the official in protest.

Up on the massive TitanTron, the production truck immediately rolled the slow-motion replay for the live crowd.

Fourteen thousand people watched the screen as the referee's hand came down for the first count. *ONE.* Avalanche was struggling. *TWO.* Avalanche flexed his massive core to bridge up.

*THREE.* Literally a microsecond after the referee's hand made contact with the mat, Avalanche's right shoulder violently surged off the canvas. It was incredibly close, but the visual evidence was undeniable: the referee's hand hit the mat first. The decision was final.

The crowd erupted all over again, laughing at the giant's expense.

At the top of the stage, Craig Bronson pointed a single finger down at the ring, laughing breathlessly as the Portland crowd chanted his name.

Inside the ring, Avalanche let out another guttural roar of fury. He violently grabbed the top rope, shaking the entire 20-by-20 ring structure in a terrifying display of raw strength. Finnley was desperately trying to calm his giant down, yelling at him that they still had a job to do later tonight.

I looked up at the digital clock suspended above the stage. It was 7:40 PM.

The pyrotechnic ban was on the line. The AWE World Heavyweight Championship was on the line. Team Sterling was officially cracking.

The main event was next.

### Part 11: The Main Event Recap – 7:45 PM (PST)

After the sheer, unadulterated chaos of Craig Bronson’s massive upset over Avalanche, the Rose City Center finally took a collective breath.

The ringside crew finished clearing the remnants of the broken instruments from the stage, and the house lights dipped to a low, moody blue to signal the witching hour. We had reached the top of the broadcast. The main event was imminent.

Without the commentary desk to guide us through the television transition, the live audience relied entirely on the massive TitanTron above the entrance stage. The screen cut to black, and a low, dramatic, synthesized heartbeat began pulsing through the PA system.

Even through our heavy isolation headphones, the deep frequency of the heartbeat vibrated steadily against my collarbone.

LAST WEEK.

The text flashed across the fifty-foot screen. The video package showed Marcus Sterling standing in the ring from the previous Monday. The production truck had deliberately desaturated the color grading, washing out the bright arena lights to make the General Manager look even colder and more clinical.

"Effective immediately, all arena pyrotechnics are banned from AWE programming," Sterling's voice echoed through the arena, perfectly synced to the footage. "Welcome to the bottom line."

The Portland crowd immediately showered the screen in heavy, sustained boos as a rapid-fire montage showed furious fans, intercut with the heavy, echoing slam of a massive steel vault door.

TONIGHT.

The audio track instantly shifted gears, dropping the heartbeat for a heavy, driving, high-BPM rock instrumental. The footage cut to the opening segment we had witnessed just under two hours ago. Sterling was in the ring right here in Portland, soaking in the nuclear heat.

"You wanted the explosions..." Sterling sneered on the tape. "Well, my first official act was to ban the pyrotechnics because you don't deserve them!"

The sound of shattering glass tore through the speakers. Jaxson Vance’s heavy grunge theme hit the video package. The screen showed Vance marching down the ramp, staring daggers right through the corporate executive.

Sitting next to me, Rachel was completely mesmerized by the masterfully edited hype video. She had her foam finger resting in her lap, her eyes glued to the TitanTron, watching the narrative pieces snap together.

"You hide behind Silas Thorne's World Championship to protect your precious bottom line..." Vance growled on the video. "Put Silas Thorne in this ring with me. Tonight."

Sterling was shown laughing nervously, shaking his head.

"If Silas beats me, my team will forfeit at No Quarter, and you can keep your cheap, pathetic pyro ban forever," Vance countered, his taped finger pointing like a loaded gun. "But... when I beat Silas Thorne tonight... the stakes for No Quarter change. If your team loses this Sunday, the pyrotechnic ban is lifted forever."

The production team used stutter-cuts to emphasize the crowd going absolutely ballistic. The camera pushed in agonizingly tight on Sterling’s face, broadcasting the exact moment his corporate composure completely fractured under the pressure of his own massive ego.

"FINE!" Sterling screamed on the tape, looking completely unhinged. "Tonight's main event! Silas Thorne versus Jaxson Vance for the AWE World Championship!"

The massive screen flashed blindingly white.

A gorgeous, high-definition 3D render of the gleaming gold AWE World Heavyweight Championship spun slowly on the screen. A deep, cinematic voiceover boomed over the heavy rock instrumental, vibrating the barricade just inches from our knees.

"TONIGHT. TWO MEN. ONE CHAMPIONSHIP. AND THE FUTURE OF THE RESISTANCE."

The screen split. On the left was Jaxson "The Outlaw" Vance, looking gritty, taped-up, and fiercely determined. On the right was "The Blueprint" Silas Thorne, looking arrogant, immaculate, and furious. In the center, the AWE World Championship spun gracefully above the *No Quarter* logo.

The TitanTron faded to the live camera feed, showing the roaring crowd.

"VANCE! VANCE! VANCE! VANCE!"

The chant started immediately, rolling down from the upper deck and slamming into the ring like a physical wave. Portland was absolutely desperate to see their hometown boy win the gold and bring back the fireworks.

But sitting dead center in the front row, I realized the incredibly strange, paradoxical reality of our situation.

We loved Jaxson Vance. He was the gritty, blue-collar soul of the promotion. But if he won tonight, he would lift the pyrotechnic ban this Sunday in Seattle. He would bring the concussive, terrifying explosions back to the arenas, instantly turning the AWE back into a sensory minefield for Rachel.

To keep Rachel safe, to keep this environment accessible for her... the hometown hero had to lose.

I looked over at Kristin and Jessica. They had come to the exact same realization. Kristin grimaced, while Jessica just offered a wry, helpless smile from her chair. We were sitting in the epicenter of the Resistance, but for the sake of our sister's neurological safety, we had no choice.

We were officially rooting for the corporate villains.

### Part 12: The Main Event – 7:45 PM to 7:56 PM (PST)

The house lights dropped completely. Fourteen thousand people in the Rose City Center rose to their feet in absolute, buzzing unison.

The sound of shattering glass ripped through the PA system, immediately followed by a heavy, grinding grunge guitar riff.

The hometown pop for Jaxson "The Outlaw" Vance was so violently loud I thought the concrete floor was going to crack. Rachel and I instantly slid our heavy, over-ear isolation headphones firmly into place. The thick foam shells neutralized the concussive, deafening wall of sound from the screaming Oregonians, turning the overwhelming noise into a manageable, muffled roar so we could visually enjoy the adrenaline rush.

Kristin leaned over, smiling widely, and threw her arms up in the air alongside Jessica as Vance stepped through the curtain. He stomped down the ramp, his eyes burning with intensity, ignoring the outstretched hands of the fans. He slid into the ring, climbed the nearest turnbuckle, and threw a taped fist into the air.

The music violently shifted to a methodical, arrogant instrumental track. The arena instantly pivoted from a deafening cheer to a wall of localized, venomous heat.

"The Blueprint" Silas Thorne stepped onto the stage. He had shed his tailored suit for his pristine, silver-and-black in-ring gear, the massive, gleaming AWE World Heavyweight Championship strapped securely around his waist. He looked infuriated, flanked on either side by the grinning Carter "The Fox" Finnley and the terrifying Avalanche.

Thorne entered the ring and took his sweet time unbuckling the championship, taunting Vance. He finally shoved the heavy gold belt into the chest of Senior Official Jack Miller.

Jack Miller was an AWE institution—a twenty-five-year veteran referee with zero tolerance for locker room politics. He famously took no crap from the talent. Miller ripped the belt out of Thorne’s hands, silencing the champion's trash talk with a stern, aggressive point of his finger.

Honoring the time-tested tradition of World Championship matches, Miller stepped to the dead center of the ring. He raised the heavy gold belt high above his head, slowly rotating clockwise to present the championship to all four sides of the arena. The sheer prestige of the gold sent another shockwave of cheers through the building.

Miller handed the belt to a ringside technician, checked both competitors, and signaled the timekeeper.

The bell rang, and Vance absolutely exploded out of the corner.

He hit Thorne with a blistering running clothesline, backing him into the ropes, and unleashed a barrage of heavy, taped fists. Thorne quickly bailed out of the ring to catch his breath, but Vance was on him in a second, slamming the champion's head repeatedly into the hood of the ringside broadcast desk.

The match was a grueling, twelve-minute clash of styles. Thorne utilized his surgical technical prowess, grounding the brawler with brutal submission holds and violently targeting Vance's knees to take away his explosive power. But Vance was running on pure hometown adrenaline. Every single time Thorne thought he had him put away, Vance fought out of the hold, fueled by the localized thunder of the Portland crowd.

At exactly 7:52 PM, the climax hit.

Vance executed a massive back-body drop. Thorne scrambled to his feet, desperate to regain control. He attempted a wild, spinning heel kick, but Vance read it perfectly and ducked.

Thorne’s heavy boot connected cleanly with the side of Senior Official Jack Miller's head. The veteran referee collapsed to the canvas, completely unconscious.

The arena descended into absolute chaos.

With the official down, Avalanche and Carter Finnley immediately jumped onto the ring apron to attack Vance. But the Resistance had made a promise in the locker room.

Sprinting down the long entrance ramp came "Iron" Tommy Brooks and the Midnight Runners. They didn't even slow down; they violently yanked Finnley off the apron, initiating a massive, swinging brawl on the thin mats of the arena floor.

Inside the ring, Avalanche slid through the ropes, preparing to Chokeslam Vance.

Suddenly, a figure vaulted over the heavy steel barricade from the crowd area directly next to Kristin and Rachel. It was the masked wildcard, Fracture.

Fracture scrambled onto the ring apron, grabbed Avalanche from behind, and violently pulled the 320-pound giant completely out of the ring. The momentum carried both men crashing to the floor, sending Avalanche spine-first into the heavy steel ring steps with a sickening, unamplified *CRUNCH* that vibrated straight through our folding chairs.

Sitting inches away, I planted my heavy boots into the concrete and gripped the metal frame of Jessica's chair, acting as an unshakable physical anchor as the massive bodies flew around our perimeter. Rachel's eyes were wide, safely shielded from the deafening crowd noise but perfectly absorbing the raw, kinetic energy of the brawl happening just feet away.

With the ringside area turning into a literal warzone, General Manager Marcus Sterling sprinted down the ramp in a sheer panic. He grabbed the AWE World Championship belt from the timekeeper's table and slid it across the canvas to Silas Thorne.

Thorne gripped the heavy gold plates. Vance turned around, groggy. Thorne swung the championship like a baseball bat, aiming squarely for Vance's skull.

But Vance dropped to his knees.

The heavy gold plates of the championship belt sailed entirely over Vance's head and smashed violently into the taut top ring rope. The kinetic energy had nowhere to go but backward. The rope violently rebounded, snapping the heavy belt back like a rubber band and smacking Silas Thorne directly between the eyes with a sickening *THWACK*.

Thorne dropped the belt, stumbling backward, completely dazed.

Vance bounced off the opposite ropes, built terrifying speed, and hit his signature finishing maneuver—The Outlaw Lariat. It nearly decapitated the champion. Thorne hit the mat, lifeless.

Vance scrambled to hook the leg.

Slowly, groggily, Senior Official Jack Miller began to stir. The Portland crowd, recognizing the absolute magnitude of the moment, started counting along at the top of their lungs.

Miller crawled over, slamming his hand onto the canvas.

ONE!

TWO!

THREE!

The timekeeper hammered the bell frantically, and Vance's heavy grunge theme exploded through the PA system. The Rose City Center achieved maximum auditory capacity. It was an earth-shattering, concussive wave of pure joy.

Rachel and I pressed our hands firmly against the outside shells of our isolation headphones, grinning from ear to ear as the localized noise shook the building to its foundation. Inside the ring, Jack Miller retrieved the championship from the canvas and handed it to a bleeding, breathless Jaxson Vance. Vance held the gold high in the air, climbing the turnbuckle as his hometown worshipped him.

The Resistance had won. The corporate machine had been shattered on free television.

Jaxson “The Outlaw” Vance stood tall in the ring, the referee raising his hand as the crowd roared around us. No commentary in our ears—just the noise, the lights, the feeling that something had shifted.

I glanced up at the digital clock hanging above the stage.

7:56 PM.

That’s when it sank in.

This wasn’t about the title changing hands tonight—Vance hadn’t taken it.\
He’d taken *control*.

People around me started piecing it together at the same time—phones coming out, chatter spreading seat to seat, the implications hitting in waves.

By winning tonight, The Resistance had earned the right to *add a stipulation* to Sunday’s match.

And as the realization spread through the arena, it turned that victory into something far more dangerous:

If The Resistance wins this Sunday… the pyrotechnics ban is lifted.

You could feel the reaction ripple outward. Not just cheers—something sharper than that. Anticipation. Unease.

Because everyone in that building knew what it meant.

No restrictions.\
No corporate limits.

Just fire… waiting for a trigger.

And come Sunday in Seattle, that trigger might be The Resistance.

### Part 13: The Rampage & The Ultimatum – 7:56 PM to 8:00 PM (PST)

The Rose City Center was completely unglued. The visual of Jaxson Vance holding the AWE World Heavyweight Championship high above his head sent the Portland crowd into an absolute frenzy.

Down in the front row, the sheer volume was physically staggering. Rachel and I immediately reached up, clamping our hands tightly over the hard plastic shells of our isolation headphones, pressing them firmly against our ears to seal out -the concussive, deafening wave of noise. Despite the intense sensory spike, we were both beaming, completely caught up in the pure, localized ecstasy of the title change.

Inside the ring, Vance knew his window to celebrate was incredibly short. He pointed to the Portland crowd, kissed the gold center plate of the championship, and rolled quickly under the bottom rope.

But he didn't go up the entrance ramp. He knew exactly what was waiting for him back there. Instead, Vance hopped the heavy steel barricade on the far side of the arena, holding the belt high as he disappeared into a sea of grabbing, cheering fans, safely escaping through the concourse.

Inside the ring, Silas Thorne slowly rolled onto his stomach. He was groggy, clutching his forehead exactly where the heavy gold plates of the championship belt had struck him.

He pushed himself up to his knees and looked around the ring. His waist was empty. He looked toward the entrance ramp. Nothing. Finally, his eyes tracked to the far side of the arena, where the tail end of Vance's chaotic exit through the crowd was still visible.

The realization hit him like a freight train. He lost the title. He lost the pyro ban.

Silas Thorne completely snapped.

His face contorted into a mask of pure, unhinged rage. He spun around and saw Senior Official Jack Miller slowly trying to stand up in the corner. Thorne charged. He grabbed the twenty-five-year veteran official by the collar and violently hurled him through the middle ropes, sending the referee crashing to the floor.

Thorne slid out of the ring, his eyes wild. He marched directly toward the broadcast desk.

Even without hearing the commentary, we could see the panic. Cal Stone scrambled backward out of his chair, retreating into the crowd for his life. But Dallas Ward wasn't fast enough. Thorne reached across the desk, grabbed the legendary play-by-play announcer by the lapels of his suit jacket, and effortlessly hurled him completely over the broadcast table. Dallas crashed into a heap of cables and heavy monitors with a sickening thud.

But Thorne wasn't finished.

He grabbed Dallas Ward's heavy, steel-framed broadcast chair and hurled it violently against the steel ring post.

CLANG!

He marched down the ringside perimeter, breathing heavily, the veins visibly bulging in his neck. He grabbed a folded steel chair from the timekeeper's area and threw it with blinding speed against the steel barricade, directly in front of our section.

CRASH!

For us, the sensory experience was terrifyingly real. The unamplified, metallic screech of the steel chairs hitting the barricade and the concrete floor cut right through the ambient crowd noise. Instinctively, I squared my shoulders, planting my boots into the floor and standing firm as a physical buffer between the flying hardware and Jessica’s wheelchair. I didn't move an inch in the face of Thorne’s violent tantrum, and Jessica just watched calmly, completely trusting the anchor.

Thorne kicked the heavy steel ring steps apart, sending the massive top half crashing to the floor. He stood amidst the absolute wreckage of the ringside area, his chest heaving, his silver-and-black gear stained with sweat.

Slowly, he turned his head toward the entrance stage.

Standing halfway up the long ramp was General Manager Marcus Sterling.

Sterling’s expensive suit was completely disheveled. He was backing away slowly, his hands raised in a defensive posture, looking absolutely terrified of the monster he had just unleashed by booking this match.

Thorne locked eyes with his boss. It was a look of pure, unadulterated murder. The former champion pointed a single, trembling finger squarely at Sterling, silently and intensely blaming the executive for the catastrophic decision to put the belt on the line.

Sterling stopped retreating. He realized his corporate empire was completely crumbling on live television. Desperate to assert whatever shred of authority he had left, Sterling snatched a microphone from a terrified production assistant near the stage.

He raised the mic. His hands were visibly shaking.

"You are out of control, Thorne!" Sterling yelled, his voice cracking violently as it echoed through the arena. "You have one chance to fix this! If you cost Team Sterling the match this Sunday... YOU ARE SUSPENDED INDEFINITELY!"

Thorne's eyes widened at the threat. He took a slow, menacing step toward the ramp as the crowd roared at the massive ultimatum.

Sterling dropped the microphone, turned, and sprinted through the curtain, fleeing his own champion.

The digital clock above the stage hit 7:59 PM.

Silas Thorne stood completely alone at ringside. He was breathing like a caged animal, surrounded by the physical destruction he had just caused, staring a hole through the empty entrance stage.

Above him, the massive TitanTron flashed a final, ominous graphic: *AWE NO QUARTER – LIVE THIS SUNDAY ON PAY-PER-VIEW.*

The arena plunged into total darkness. The broadcast was over.

### Part 14: The House Lights & The Journey Home – 8:00 PM (PST)

The digital clock suspended above the stage clicked to 8:00 PM. The TitanTron faded to black, the PA system cut out, and an instant later, the massive, blindingly bright white house lights of the Rose City Center slammed on.

Just like that, the illusion evaporated. The war zone was gone, replaced by the chaotic reality of 14,000 exhausted fans shuffling toward the exits.

Down in the front row, our adrenaline was crashing hard. We had survived the gauntlet, but we couldn't leave just yet. AWE ringside seats aren't just tickets; they are heavy, padded souvenir folding chairs. But for safety reasons during the broadcast, they were heavily clipped and zip-tied together to prevent them from becoming projectiles. We waited patiently as the ringside technicians, armed with heavy wire cutters, moved down the row, unhooking our section.

Once the chairs were finally free, I knelt down in front of Jessica. She wrapped her arms securely around my neck, and I hoisted her up into a piggy-back carry. The second her weight settled onto my shoulders, a sharp, white-hot spike of pain shot directly up my lower spine. My back was already completely wrecked from carrying her down the concrete at the start of the show, and the tight, unyielding spasticity in my legs was screaming in protest.

I gritted my teeth, carefully walking her out of the tight seating row and lowering her gently into her own custom wheelchair parked nearby.

The moment her weight left me, my knees buckled.

"Michael!" Kristin gasped, stepping forward instantly.

I couldn't stand up straight. The muscles in my lower back had completely locked up into a solid, agonizing knot.

"I've got you," Rachel said fiercely, stepping up to my left side. Kristin immediately moved to my right.

I slung my heavy arms over both of their shoulders, letting the two women take the brunt of my physical weight. It was a complete role reversal; after spending the entire night acting as their physical anchor and shield against the chaos of the arena, my sister and her best friend were now physically carrying me.

Jessica balanced our massive VIP Prize Pack on her lap—the exclusive duffel bags, signed programs, and Rachel's prized foam finger—while Rachel and Kristin stabilized me.

"Alright," a familiar, gravelly voice called out over the dull roar of the departing crowd. "Let's get you guys out of this madhouse."

Walking down the aisle toward us was Dave "The Arsenal" Cross. He gave us a warm, tired smile, taking in our battered, exhausted formation, and gestured for us to follow him. Because navigating a wheelchair against the tide of 14,000 people pushing toward the main concourse stairs was a logistical nightmare, Dave personally escorted us through the backstage curtain to use the arena's heavy-duty freight elevator.

As we rolled through the backstage area, Dave fell into step right next to Rachel, who was currently acting as my left crutch.

"Hey, kiddo," Dave said gently, looking at Rachel. "I didn't forget. I just got off the phone with the merchandise department. Your Arsenal Skull t-shirt and hat? The order is officially in. The boys back in Stamford are going to process it first thing tomorrow morning."

Rachel stopped dead in her tracks, forcing Kristin and me to halt with her. She looked up at her absolute hero, her eyes wide with sheer disbelief.

Dave chuckled, patting her gently on the shoulder. "Don't cry on me now, kid. You've got a Pay-Per-View to get ready for this Sunday."

We reached the massive freight elevator, the hydraulic motor humming loudly as it carried us up to the street-level concourse.

When the heavy metal doors slid open, we were officially past the restricted backstage area and back in the public zone. We stopped to thank Dave profusely, shaking his hand and saying our goodbyes. A single tear of pure, unadulterated joy spilled over Rachel's eyelashes and rolled down her cheek. She was actually going to get the gear, and it was going to be shipped straight to our new front door in Renton, Washington.

We pushed through the glass exit doors out into the crisp, cold Portland night air.

Right across the plaza, the Rose City Center CTS station was an absolute madhouse.

The platforms stretched out beneath the shadow of the US-30 overpass, all harsh fluorescent light and concrete—but there was barely any platform left to see anymore. It was just people. A dense, shifting wall of bodies packed shoulder-to-shoulder from the tracks all the way out onto the sidewalks. Every few minutes, a train rolled in already full, doors sliding open to a wave of frustration as maybe three or four people squeezed in while everyone else got left behind.

“Thank God we don’t have to get on that,” Kristin muttered beside us.

I didn’t argue. I just tightened my grip on the handles and angled us right, aiming to skirt past the whole mess.

That plan lasted about ten feet.

The crowd had spilled way beyond the station, clogging the sidewalk in a slow, uneven current. I eased my pace immediately, nudging Jessica’s chair forward inch by inch, watching for gaps that barely existed. From up here, I could at least see over most of it—but that didn’t make it easier to move through.

“Excuse us—wheelchair coming through,” Rachel called, a step ahead, trying to clear a path.

Some people heard her. Some didn’t.

A guy in a wrestling hoodie took a step backward without looking, right into us. I braked hard, the footrests stopping just short of his heel.

“Oh—shoot, sorry!” he blurted, jumping out of the way. That little moment of awareness rippled outward—people shifting, turning, making space—but always just a second too late.

“Thanks,” I muttered, already guiding us into the next opening before it disappeared.

Another train screeched in under the overpass. Like a reflex, the entire crowd leaned toward it, compressing the sidewalk even more. For a second, we were boxed in on all sides.

I leaned down a little. “You good?” I asked quietly.

Jessica gave the smallest nod.

“Alright. I’ve got you.”

Short pushes. Stop. Pivot. Slip through. Repeat.

From where she sat, I knew it had to feel even tighter—nothing but legs and coats and bags, the noise bouncing off the concrete overhead. Up here, it was loud enough. Down there, it had to be overwhelming.

“Wheelchair—coming through, please,” Rachel tried again, firmer this time.

This time more people reacted quickly, stepping aside, holding their spots just long enough for us to pass.

We kept moving.

And then, almost without warning, it broke.

Half a block past the station, the pressure just… vanished. The crowd thinned into loose clusters, the noise dropped off, and suddenly I had space again—real space—to breathe and steer without fighting for every inch.

I glanced up—and there it was.

The Crown Regency. Glass front, bright lobby lights, calm like none of that chaos even existed.

“Almost there,” I said, more to myself than anyone else.

A minute later, we rolled straight up to the entrance, leaving the entire mess behind us like it was another world.

We took the elevator up to the third floor, dragging our souvenir chairs and VIP bags down the quiet, carpeted hallway to our ADA-accessible room.

The moment the heavy wooden door clicked shut behind us, the silence felt absolutely luxurious. The room featured two plush queen-sized beds with wide clearances for Jessica's chair.

"Easy now," Kristin instructed gently.

Rachel and Kristin carefully navigated me toward the bed nearest the window. Moving with perfectly synchronized teamwork, they slowly lowered me down until my back finally hit the soft mattress. I let out a long, agonizing groan of relief as the pressure was finally taken off my spine.

Jessica parked her chair near the foot of the beds, shedding her jacket with a tired sigh.

I barely had time to kick my boots off before the mattress dipped beside me. Rachel didn't even bother unpacking her VIP bag. She climbed right into the bed next to me, fully clothed, and instantly curled herself against my side.

She rested her head heavy on my chest, draping her arm over my ribs. The massive sensory and emotional high of the wrestling show was finally catching up to her processor, and she desperately needed her anchor to decompress.

I winced slightly through the pain in my back, but I wrapped my arm around her shoulders anyway, holding her tight. We had survived the Monday Night WAR, and the pack was exactly where it belonged.

# Book 17: A Few Small Repairs

## Chapter 1: Breakfast in Vancouver (Tuesday, November 21, 2006)

### Part 1: The Anchor's Rest – 7:00 AM (PST)

Waking up was not a slow, gradual process. It was a sudden, blinding spike of white-hot agony.

I opened my eyes, staring up at the generic, textured ceiling of the Crown Regency hotel room. I tried to shift my weight on the queen-sized mattress, and my lower back immediately locked up in a violent, paralyzing spasm. The toll of piggy-backing Jessica down the concrete arena steps, completely absorbing the shock of the Cruiserweight barricade crash, and then hauling her back out of the Rose City Center had finally come due.

I let out a sharp, involuntary hiss of pain through my teeth.

The sound woke Rachel. She was curled tightly against my side, her head resting on my chest. She blinked her dark eyes open, the grogginess clearing in a fraction of a second as her hyper-observant radar detected my distress.

She pushed herself up on one elbow, looking down at me.

"My back," I rasped, barely able to draw a full breath. "It's completely locked."

Rachel didn't panic. She didn't freeze. For my entire life, I had been her physical and emotional anchor, absorbing her meltdowns, shielding her from the crowds, and holding her together when the world threatened to tear her apart.

Now, the physics had shifted. The anchor was broken. It was her turn.
